revision.text:
{{nihongo|'''Suiten-gū'''|水天宮}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in [[Chūō, Tokyo]], [[Japan]]. It is devoted to conception and safe childbirth. In 1818 the ninth [[daimyo]] of the [[Kurume Domain]] established the Suiten-gū in [[Edo]] as a branch of a shrine of the same name in [[Kurume, Fukuoka]]. It was inside the grounds of the domain's mansion in the Mita district of what is now [[Minato, Tokyo]], and the domain opened it to the public on the fifth day of every month. In 1871, the [[Arima clan|Arima family]] moved from Mita to [[Akasaka, Tokyo|Akasaka]], taking the shrine with it, and in the following year they moved the shrine to its present location, on a site that had been the family's middle mansion.
There are about 25 other shrines of the same name in Japan.
[[Suitengumae Station]] is close to this shrine and takes its name from it.
==See also== *[[Kurume Suiten-gū]]
==External links== *[http://www.suitengu.or.jp/ Official site] (in Japanese)
{{coord|35|41|01|N|139|47|06|E|region:JP_scale:30000_source:jawiki|display=title}}

In 1818 the ninth [[daimyo]] of the [[Kurume Domain]] established the Suiten-gū in [[Edo]] as a branch of a shrine of the same name in [[Kurume, Fukuoka]]. It was inside the grounds of the domain's mansion in the Mita district of what is now [[Minato, Tokyo]], and the domain opened it to the public on the fifth day of every month. In 1871, the [[Arima clan|Arima family]] moved from Mita to [[Akasaka, Tokyo|Akasaka]], taking the shrine with it, and in the following year they moved the shrine to its present location, on a site that had been the family's middle mansion. There are about 25 other shrines of the same name in Japan. [[Tokyo]] 103-0014 | phone = | website = http://www.suitengu.or.jp/ }} {{nihongo|'''Suiten-gū'''|水天宮}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in [[Chūō, Tokyo]], [[Japan]]. It is devoted to conception and safe childbirth. In 1818 the ninth [[daimyo]] of the [[Kurume Domain]] established the Suiten-gū in [[Edo]] as a branch of a shrine of the same name in [[Kurume, Fukuoka]]. It was inside the grounds of the domain's mansion in the Mita district of what is now [[Minato, Tokyo]], and the domain opened it to the public on the fifth day of every month. In 1871, the [[Arima clan|Arima family]] moved from Mita to [[Akasaka, Tokyo|Akasaka]], taking the shrine with it, and in the following year they moved the shrine to its present location, on a site that had been the family's middle mansion. In Suitengu,[[Varuna]] was also deified. In the 19th century, Varuna was changed to [[Amenominakanushi]]. The name was changed. Because,Varuna of Zoroastrian,and Japanese Amenominakanushi,were regarded as the same one. There are about 25 other shrines of the same name in Japan. Suitengumae Station is close to this shrine and takes its name from it. There are about 25 other shrines of the same name in Japan. {{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Suiten-gū
水天宮 | image = Suitengu-tokyo-winter2014.jpg | caption = The current (temporary) ''[[Haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'' | width = | type = | dedication = [[Amenominakanushi]]（as [[Varuna]]）
[[Emperor Antoku]]
[[Taira no Tokuko]]
[[Taira no Tokiko]] | founded = 1818 | closed = | founder = | priest = | address = 2-4-1 Nihonbashi-Kakigarachō, [[Chūō, Tokyo|Chūō]]
[[Tokyo]] 103-0014 | phone = | website = http://www.suitengu.or.jp/ }} The meaning of {{nihongo|'''Suiten-gū'''|水天宮}} is a shrine of Varuna.Varuna had been called "Suiten" in [[Japanese buddhism]].It's a [[Shinto shrine]] in [[Chūō, Tokyo]], [[Japan]]. It is devoted to conception and safe childbirth. In 1818 the ninth [[daimyo]] of the [[Kurume Domain]] established the Suiten-gū in [[Edo]] as a branch of a shrine of the same name in [[Kurume, Fukuoka]]. It was inside the grounds of the domain's mansion in the Mita district of what is now [[Minato, Tokyo]], and the domain opened it to the public on the fifth day of every month. In 1871, the [[Arima clan|Arima family]] moved from Mita to [[Akasaka, Tokyo|Akasaka]], taking the shrine with it, and in the following year they moved the shrine to its present location, on a site that had been the family's middle mansion. In Suitengu,[[Varuna]] was also deified. For [[Shinbutsu_bunri]] in the 19th century, Varuna was changed to [[Amenominakanushi]]. The name was changed. Because,Varuna of Zoroastrian,and Japanese Amenominakanushi,were regarded as the same one. There are about 25 other shrines of the same name in Japan.Holy days of suiten-gū(the days good for going to a suiten-gū) are "the 5th day" of every month and "day of a dog". (This dog is a dog({{nihongo|'''inu'''|戌}}) of [[Chinese_zodiac]].) [[Suitengumae Station]] is close to this shrine and takes its name from it. ==See also== *[[Kurume Suiten-gū]] ==External links== *[http://www.suitengu.or.jp/ Official site] (in Japanese) {{coord|35|41|01|N|139|47|06|E|region:JP_scale:30000_source:jawiki|display=title}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Suiten-gu (Tokyo)}} [[Category:Chūō, Tokyo]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]] {{Shinto-stub}}

{{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Suiten-gū
水天宮 | image = Suitengu-tokyo-winter2014.jpg | caption = The current (temporary) ''[[Haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'' | width = | type = | dedication = [[Amenominakanushi]]（as [[Varuna]]）
[[Emperor Antoku]]
[[Taira no Tokuko]]
[[Taira no Tokiko]] | founded = 1818 | closed = | founder = | priest = | address = 2-4-1 Nihonbashi-Kakigarachō, [[Chūō, Tokyo|Chūō]]
[[Tokyo]] 103-0014 | phone = | website = http://www.suitengu.or.jp/ }} The meaning of {{nihongo|'''Suiten-gū'''|水天宮}} is a shrine of Varuna. Varuna had been called "Suiten" in [[Japanese buddhism]]. He is one of the twelve Devas, as guardian deities, who are found in or around Buddhist shrines ([[:ja:十二天|Jūni-ten]], 十二天).[http://www.emuseum.jp/detail/100031/000/000?mode=detail&d_lang=en Twelve Heavenly Deities (Devas)] Nara National Museum, Japan{{refn|group=note|Varuna joins these other eleven Devas of Buddhism, found in Japan and other parts of southeast Asia: Indra (Taishaku-ten), Agni (Ka-ten), Yama (Emma-ten), Nirrti (Rasetsu-ten), Vayu (Fu-ten), Ishana (Ishana-ten), Kubera (Tamon-ten), Varuna (Sui-ten) Brahma (Bon-ten), Prithvi (Chi-ten), Surya (Nit-ten), Chandra (Gat-ten).Willem Frederik Stutterheim et al (1995), Rāma-legends and Rāma-reliefs in Indonesia, ISBN 978-8170172512, pages xiv-xviS Biswas (2000), Art of Japan, Northern, ISBN 978-8172112691, page 184Adrian Snodgrass (2007), The Symbolism of the Stupa, Motilal Banarsidass, ISBN 978-8120807815, pages 120-124, 298-300}} Suitengu is a [[Shinto shrine]] in [[Chūō, Tokyo]], [[Japan]]. It is devoted to conception and safe childbirth. In 1818 the ninth [[daimyo]] of the [[Kurume Domain]] established the Suiten-gū in [[Edo]] as a branch of a shrine of the same name in [[Kurume, Fukuoka]]. It was inside the grounds of the domain's mansion in the Mita district of what is now [[Minato, Tokyo]], and the domain opened it to the public on the fifth day of every month. In 1871, the [[Arima clan|Arima family]] moved from Mita to [[Akasaka, Tokyo|Akasaka]], taking the shrine with it, and in the following year they moved the shrine to its present location, on a site that had been the family's middle mansion.https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E6%B0%B4%E5%A4%A9%E5%AE%AE In Suitengu, [[Varuna]] was also deified. For [[Shinbutsu_bunri]], when Shinto deities and Buddhist deities were separated, Varuna was changed to [[Amenominakanushi]]."Tokyo Suitengu monogatari" 1985 Kodansha, ISBN-10:406202117X There are about 25 other shrines of the same name in Japan. [[Suitengumae Station]] is close to this shrine and takes its name from it. ==See also== *[[Kurume Suiten-gū]] ==Notes== {{reflist|group=note}} ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== *[http://www.suitengu.or.jp/ Official site] (in Japanese) {{coord|35|41|01|N|139|47|06|E|region:JP_scale:30000_source:jawiki|display=title}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Suiten-gu (Tokyo)}} [[Category:Chūō, Tokyo]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]] {{Shinto-stub}}

It is devoted to conception and safe childbirth. In 1818 the ninth [[daimyo]] of the [[Kurume Domain]] established the Suiten-gū in [[Edo]] as a branch of a shrine of the same name in [[Kurume, Fukuoka]]. It was inside the grounds of the domain's mansion in the Mita district of what is now [[Minato, Tokyo]], and the domain opened it to the public on the fifth day of every month. In 1871, the [[Arima clan|Arima family]] moved from Mita to [[Akasaka, Tokyo|Akasaka]], taking the shrine with it, and in the following year they moved the shrine to its present location, on a site that had been the family's middle mansion.https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E6%B0%B4%E5%A4%A9%E5%AE%AE In Suitengu, [[Varuna]] was also deified. For [[Shinbutsu bunri]], when Shinto deities and Buddhist deities were separated, Varuna was changed to [[Amenominakanushi]]."Tokyo Suitengu monogatari" 1985 Kodansha, ISBN 406202117X There are about 25 other shrines of the same name in Japan. [[Suitengumae Station]] is close to this shrine and takes its name from it. ==See also== *[[Kurume Suiten-gū]] ==Notes== {{reflist|group=note}} ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== *[http://www.suitengu.or.jp/ Official site] (in Japanese) {{coord|35|41|01|N|139|47|06|E|region:JP_scale:30000_source:jawiki|display=title}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Suiten-gu (Tokyo)}} [[Category:Chūō, Tokyo]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]] {{Shinto-stub}}

{{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Suiten-gū

[[Tokyo]] 103-0014 | phone = | website = http://www.suitengu.or.jp/ }} {{nihongo|'''Suiten-gū'''|水天宮}}, literally "Palace of the Watery Sky", or "Palace of Suiten", ia s [[Shinto shrine]] dedicated to Suiten, the Japanese name of the deity of [[Hindu]] origins [[Varuna]].{{refn|group=note|Varuna is one among a number of Hindu deities incorporated into [[Shinto]]: Indra (Jap. ''Taishakuten''), Agni (''Katen''), Yama (''Emmaten''), Nirrti (''Rasetsuten''), Vayu (''Futen''), Ishana (''Ishanaten''), Kubera (''Tamonten''), Brahma (''Bonten''), Prithvi (''Chiten''), Surya (''Nitten''), Chandra (''Gatten'').Willem Frederik Stutterheim et al (1995), Rāma-legends and Rāma-reliefs in Indonesia, {{ISBN|978-8170172512}}, pages xiv-xviS Biswas (2000), Art of Japan, Northern, {{ISBN|978-8172112691}}, page 184Adrian Snodgrass (2007), The Symbolism of the Stupa, Motilal Banarsidass, {{ISBN|978-8120807815}}, pages 120-124, 298-300}} Suitengu is located in [[Chūō, Tokyo]], [[Japan]]. It is devoted to conception and safe childbirth. In 1818 the ninth [[daimyo]] of the [[Kurume Domain]] established the Suitengu in [[Edo]] as a branch of a shrine of the same name in [[Kurume, Fukuoka]]. It was inside the grounds of the domain's mansion in the Mita district of what is now [[Minato, Tokyo]], and the domain opened it to the public on the fifth day of every month. In 1871, the [[Arima clan|Arima family]] moved from Mita to [[Akasaka, Tokyo|Akasaka]], taking the shrine with them, and in the following year they moved the shrine to its present location, on a site that had been occupied by one of the family's mansions. When the empire enforsed the ''[[Shinbutsu bunri]]'', the official separation of Shinto shrines and Buddhist temples, Varuna was identified with the Japanese supreme God, [[Amenominakanushi]]."Tokyo Suitengu monogatari" 1985 Kodansha, {{ISBN|406202117X}} [[Suitengumae Station]] is close to this shrine and takes its name from it. There are about twenty-five other shrines of the same name in Japan. ==See also== *[[Kurume Suiten-gū]] *[[Suijin]] ==Notes== {{reflist|group=note}} ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== *[http://www.suitengu.or.jp/ Official site] (in Japanese) {{coord|35|41|01|N|139|47|06|E|region:JP_scale:30000_source:jawiki|display=title}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Suiten-gu (Tokyo)}} [[Category:Chūō, Tokyo]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]] {{Shinto-stub}}

revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building | name = Suiten-gū
水天宮 | image = Tokyo Suitengu 201604a.jpg | caption = The current shrine (Rebuilt in 2016) | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|41|01|N|139|47|06|E|region:JP_scale:30000_source:jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Amenominakanushi]]（as [[Varuna]]）
[[Emperor Antoku]]
[[Taira no Tokuko]]
[[Taira no Tokiko]] | founded_by = | established = 1818 | date_destroyed = | location = 2-4-1 Nihonbashi-Kakigarachō, [[Chūō, Tokyo|Chūō]]
[[Tokyo]] 103-0014 | website = {{URL|http://www.suitengu.or.jp/}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Suiten-gū'''|水天宮}}, literally "Palace of the Watery Sky", or "Palace of Suiten", is a [[Shinto shrine]] dedicated to Suiten, the [[Japan]]ese name of the deity of [[Hindu]] origins [[Varuna]], one of a series of Hindu deities whose worship entered Japan together with [[Buddhism]].{{refn|group=note|Varuna is one among a number of Hindu deities incorporated into [[Shinto]], introduced into Japan together with Buddhism: Indra (Jap. ''Taishakuten''), Agni (''Katen''), Yama (''Emmaten''), Nirrti (''Rasetsuten''), Vayu (''Futen''), Ishana (''Ishanaten''), Kubera (''Tamonten''), Brahma (''Bonten''), Prithvi (''Chiten''), Surya (''Nitten''), Chandra (''Gatten'').Willem Frederik Stutterheim et al (1995), Rāma-legends and Rāma-reliefs in Indonesia, {{ISBN|978-8170172512}}, pages xiv–xviS Biswas (2000), Art of Japan, Northern, {{ISBN|978-8172112691}}, page 184[[Adrian Snodgrass]] (2007), The Symbolism of the Stupa, Motilal Banarsidass, {{ISBN|978-8120807815}}, pages 120-124, 298-300}} Suitengu is located in [[Chūō, Tokyo]]. It is devoted to conception and safe childbirth. In 1818 the ninth [[daimyō]] of the [[Kurume Domain]] established the Suitengu in [[Edo]] as a branch of a shrine of the same name in [[Kurume, Fukuoka]]. It was inside the grounds of the domain's mansion in the Mita district of what is now [[Minato, Tokyo]], and the domain opened it to the public on the fifth day of every month. In 1871, the [[Arima clan|Arima family]] moved from Mita to [[Akasaka, Tokyo|Akasaka]], taking the shrine with them, and in the following year they moved the shrine to its present location, on a site that had been occupied by one of the family's mansions. When the Japanese empire enforced the ''[[Shinbutsu bunri]]'', the official separation of Shinto shrines and Buddhist temples, Varuna was identified with the Japanese supreme God, [[Amenominakanushi]]."Tokyo Suitengu monogatari" 1985 Kodansha, {{ISBN|406202117X}} [[Suitengūmae Station]] is close to this shrine and takes its name from it. There are about twenty-five other shrines of the same name in Japan. ==See also== *[[Kurume Suiten-gū]] *[[Suijin]] ==Notes== {{reflist|group=note}} ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== *[http://www.suitengu.or.jp/ Official site] (in Japanese) {{Shinto shrine}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Suiten-gu (Tokyo)}} [[Category:Chūō, Tokyo]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:1818 establishments in Japan]] {{Shinto-stub}}

''Taishakuten''), Agni (''Katen''), Yama (''Emmaten''), Nirrti (''Rasetsuten''), Vayu (''Futen''), Ishana (''Ishanaten''), Kubera (''Tamonten''), Brahma (''Bonten''), Prithvi (''Chiten''), Surya (''Nitten''), Chandra (''Gatten'').Willem Frederik Stutterheim et al (1995), Rāma-legends and Rāma-reliefs in Indonesia, {{ISBN|978-8170172512}}, pages xiv–xviS Biswas (2000), Art of Japan, Northern, {{ISBN|978-8172112691}}, page 184[[Adrian Snodgrass]] (2007), The Symbolism of the Stupa, Motilal Banarsidass, {{ISBN|978-8120807815}}, pages 120-124, 298-300}} Suitengu is located in [[Chūō, Tokyo]]. It is devoted to conception and safe childbirth. In 1818 the ninth [[daimyō]] of the [[Kurume Domain]] established the Suitengu in [[Edo]] as a branch of a shrine of the same name in [[Kurume, Fukuoka]]. It was inside the grounds of the domain's mansion in the Mita district of what is now [[Minato, Tokyo]], and the domain opened it to the public on the fifth day of every month. In 1871, the [[Arima clan|Arima family]] moved from Mita to [[Akasaka, Tokyo|Akasaka]], taking the shrine with them, and in the following year they moved the shrine to its present location, on a site that had been occupied by one of the family's mansions. When the Japanese empire enforced the ''[[Shinbutsu bunri]]'', the official separation of Shinto shrines and Buddhist temples, Varuna was identified with the Japanese supreme God, [[Amenominakanushi]]."Tokyo Suitengu monogatari" 1985 Kodansha, {{ISBN|406202117X}} [[Suitengūmae Station]] is close to this shrine and takes its name from it. There are about twenty-five other shrines of the same name in Japan. ==See also== *[[Kurume Suiten-gū]] *[[Suijin]] ==Notes== {{reflist|group=note}} ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== *[http://www.suitengu.or.jp/ Official site] (in Japanese) {{Shinto shrine}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Suiten-gu (Tokyo)}} [[Category:1818 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Chūō, Tokyo]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]] {{Shinto-stub}}

{{Infobox religious building | name = Suiten-gū
水天宮 | image = Tokyo Suitengu 201604a.jpg | caption = The current shrine (Rebuilt in 2016) | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|41|01|N|139|47|06|E|region:JP_scale:30000_source:jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Amenominakanushi]]（as [[Varuna]]）
[[Emperor Antoku]]
[[Taira no Tokuko]]
[[Taira no Tokiko]] | founded_by = | established = 1818 | date_destroyed = | location = 2-4-1 Nihonbashi-Kakigarachō, [[Chūō, Tokyo|Chūō]]
[[Tokyo]] 103-0014 | website = {{URL|http://www.suitengu.or.jp/}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Suiten-gū'''|水天宮}}, literally "Palace of the Water [[Deva (Buddhism)|Deva]]", or "Palace of Suiten", is a [[Shinto shrine]] dedicated to four deities: * [[Ame-no-Minakanushi|Amenominakanushi]] * [[Emperor Antoku|Antoku]] * [[Taira no Tokuko|Kenrenmon-in]] * [[Taira no Tokiko|Nii No Ama]] "Suiten" is the [[Japan]]ese name of the deity of [[Hindu]] origins [[Varuna]], one of a series of Hindu deities whose worship entered Japan together with [[Buddhism]].{{refn|group=note|Varuna is one among a number of Hindu deities incorporated into [[Shinto]], introduced into Japan together with Buddhism: Indra (Jap. ''Taishakuten''), Agni (''Katen''), Yama (''Emmaten''), Nirrti (''Rasetsuten''), Vayu (''Futen''), Ishana (''Ishanaten''), Kubera (''Tamonten''), Brahma (''Bonten''), Prithvi (''Chiten''), Surya (''Nitten''), Chandra (''Gatten'').Willem Frederik Stutterheim et al (1995), Rāma-legends and Rāma-reliefs in Indonesia, {{ISBN|978-8170172512}}, pages xiv–xviS Biswas (2000), Art of Japan, Northern, {{ISBN|978-8172112691}}, page 184[[Adrian Snodgrass]] (2007), The Symbolism of the Stupa, Motilal Banarsidass, {{ISBN|978-8120807815}}, pages 120-124, 298-300}}. When the Japanese Empire enforced the ''[[Shinbutsu bunri]]'', the official separation of Shinto shrines and Buddhist temples, shrines celebrating Suiten identified their dedication to [[Amenominakanushi]]."Tokyo Suitengu monogatari" 1985 Kodansha, {{ISBN|406202117X}} Suitengu is located in [[Chūō, Tokyo]]. It is devoted to conception and safe childbirth. In 1818 the ninth [[daimyō]] of the [[Kurume Domain]] established the Suitengu in [[Edo]] as a branch of a shrine of the same name in [[Kurume, Fukuoka]]. It was inside the grounds of the domain's mansion in the Mita district of what is now [[Minato, Tokyo]], and the domain opened it to the public on the fifth day of every month. In 1871, the [[Arima clan|Arima family]] moved from Mita to [[Akasaka, Tokyo|Akasaka]], taking the shrine with them, and in the following year they moved the shrine to its present location, on a site that had been occupied by one of the family's mansions. [[Suitengūmae Station]] is close to this shrine and takes its name from it. There are about twenty-five other shrines of the same name in Japan. ==See also== *[[Kurume Suiten-gū]] *[[Suijin]] ==Notes== {{reflist|group=note}} ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== *[http://www.suitengu.or.jp/ Official site] (in Japanese) {{Shinto shrine}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Suiten-gu (Tokyo)}} [[Category:1818 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Chūō, Tokyo]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]] {{Shinto-stub}}

''Taishakuten''), Agni (''Katen''), Yama (''Emmaten''), Nirrti (''Rasetsuten''), Vayu (''Futen''), Ishana (''Ishanaten''), Kubera (''Tamonten''), Brahma (''Bonten''), Prithvi (''Chiten''), Surya (''Nitten''), Chandra (''Gatten'').Willem Frederik Stutterheim et al (1995), Rāma-legends and Rāma-reliefs in Indonesia, {{ISBN|978-8170172512}}, pages xiv–xviS Biswas (2000), Art of Japan, Northern, {{ISBN|978-8172112691}}, page 184[[Adrian Snodgrass]] (2007), The Symbolism of the Stupa, Motilal Banarsidass, {{ISBN|978-8120807815}}, pages 120-124, 298-300}}. When the Japanese Empire enforced the ''[[Shinbutsu bunri]]'', the official separation of Shinto shrines and Buddhist temples, shrines celebrating Suiten identified their dedication to [[Amenominakanushi]]."Tokyo Suitengu monogatari" 1985 Kodansha, {{ISBN|406202117X}} Suitengu is located in [[Chūō, Tokyo]]. It is devoted to conception and safe childbirth. In 1818 the ninth [[daimyō]] of the [[Kurume Domain]] established the Suitengu in [[Edo]] as a branch of a shrine of the same name in [[Kurume, Fukuoka]]. It was inside the grounds of the domain's mansion in the Mita district of what is now [[Minato, Tokyo]], and the domain opened it to the public on the fifth day of every month. In 1871, the [[Arima clan|Arima family]] moved from Mita to [[Akasaka, Tokyo|Akasaka]], taking the shrine with them, and in the following year they moved the shrine to its present location, on a site that had been occupied by one of the family's mansions. [[Suitengūmae Station]] is close to this shrine and takes its name from it. There are about twenty-five other shrines of the same name in Japan. ==See also== *[[Kurume Suitengū]] *[[Suijin]] ==Notes== {{reflist|group=note}} ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== *[http://www.suitengu.or.jp/ Official site] (in Japanese) {{Chuo, Tokyo}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Suiten-gu (Tokyo)}} [[Category:1818 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Chūō, Tokyo]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]] {{Shinto-stub}}

[[Tokyo]] 103-0014 | website = {{URL|http://www.suitengu.or.jp/}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Suiten-gū'''|水天宮}}, literally "Palace of the Water [[Deva (Buddhism)|Deva]]", or "Palace of Suiten", is a [[Shinto shrine]] dedicated to four deities: * [[Ame-no-Minakanushi|Amenominakanushi]] * [[Emperor Antoku|Antoku]] * [[Taira no Tokuko|Kenrenmon-in]] * [[Taira no Tokiko|Nii No Ama]] "Suiten" is the [[Japan]]ese name of the deity of [[Hindu]] origins [[Varuna]], one of a series of Hindu deities whose worship entered Japan together with [[Buddhism]].{{refn|group=note|Varuna is one among a number of Hindu deities incorporated into [[Shinto]], introduced into Japan together with Buddhism: Indra (Jap. ''Taishakuten''), Agni (''Katen''), Yama (''Emmaten''), Nirrti (''Rasetsuten''), Vayu (''Futen''), Ishana (''Ishanaten''), Kubera (''Tamonten''), Brahma (''Bonten''), Prithvi (''Chiten''), Surya (''Nitten''), Chandra (''Gatten'').Willem Frederik Stutterheim et al (1995), Rāma-legends and Rāma-reliefs in Indonesia, {{ISBN|978-8170172512}}, pages xiv–xviS Biswas (2000), Art of Japan, Northern, {{ISBN|978-8172112691}}, page 184[[Adrian Snodgrass]] (2007), The Symbolism of the Stupa, Motilal Banarsidass, {{ISBN|978-8120807815}}, pages 120-124, 298-300}}. When the Japanese Empire enforced the ''[[Shinbutsu bunri]]'', the official separation of Shinto shrines and Buddhist temples, shrines celebrating Suiten identified their dedication to [[Amenominakanushi]]."Tokyo Suitengu monogatari" 1985 Kodansha, {{ISBN|406202117X}} Suitengu is located in [[Chūō, Tokyo]]. It is devoted to conception and safe childbirth. In 1818 the ninth [[daimyō]] of the [[Kurume Domain]] established the Suitengu in [[Edo]] as a branch of a shrine of the same name in [[Kurume, Fukuoka]]. It was inside the grounds of the domain's mansion in the Mita district of what is now [[Minato, Tokyo]], and the domain opened it to the public on the fifth day of every month. In 1871, the [[Arima clan|Arima family]] moved from Mita to [[Akasaka, Tokyo|Akasaka]], taking the shrine with them, and in the following year they moved the shrine to its present location, on a site that had been occupied by one of the family's mansions. [[Suitengūmae Station]] is close to this shrine and takes its name from it. There are about twenty-five other shrines of the same name in Japan. ==See also== *[[Kurume Suitengū]] *[[Suijin]] ==Notes== {{reflist|group=note}} ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== *[http://www.suitengu.or.jp/ Official site] (in Japanese) {{Chuo, Tokyo}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Suiten-gu (Tokyo)}} [[Category:1818 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Chūō, Tokyo]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Unranked shrines]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia4]] [[en:Suitengū (Tokyo)]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia4]] {{Shinto-stub}}

There are about twenty-five other shrines of the same name in Japan. ==See also== *[[Kurume Suitengū]] *[[Suijin]] ==Notes== {{reflist|group=note}} ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== *[http://www.suitengu.or.jp/ Official site] (in Japanese) {{Chuo, Tokyo}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Suiten-gu (Tokyo)}} [[Category:1818 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Chūō, Tokyo]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Unranked shrines]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia41]] [[en:Suitengū (Tokyo)]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia41]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia23]] [[Category:dd]] {{Shinto-stub}}

{{Infobox religious building | name = Suiten-gū
水天宮 | image = Tokyo Suitengu 201604a.jpg | caption = The current shrine (Rebuilt in 2016) | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|41|01|N|139|47|06|E|region:JP_scale:30000_source:jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Amenominakanushi]]（as [[Varuna]]）
[[Emperor Antoku]]
[[Taira no Tokuko]]
[[Taira no Tokiko]] | founded_by = | established = 1818 | date_destroyed = | location = 2-4-1 Nihonbashi-Kakigarachō, [[Chūō, Tokyo|Chūō]]
[[Tokyo]] 103-0014 | website = {{URL|http://www.suitengu.or.jp/}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Suiten-gū'''|水天宮}}, literally "Palace of the Water [[Deva (Buddhism)|Deva]]", or "Palace of Suiten", is a [[Shinto shrine]] dedicated to four deities: * [[Ame-no-Minakanushi|Amenominakanushi]] * [[Emperor Antoku|Antoku]] * [[Taira no Tokuko|Kenrenmon-in]] * [[Taira no Tokiko|Nii No Ama]] "Suiten" is the [[Japan]]ese name of the deity of [[Hindu]] origins [[Varuna]], one of a series of Hindu deities whose worship entered Japan together with [[Buddhism]].{{refn|group=note|Varuna is one among a number of Hindu deities incorporated into [[Shinto]], introduced into Japan together with Buddhism: Indra (Jap. ''Taishakuten''), Agni (''Katen''), Yama (''Emmaten''), Nirrti (''Rasetsuten''), Vayu (''Futen''), Ishana (''Ishanaten''), Kubera (''Tamonten''), Brahma (''Bonten''), Prithvi (''Chiten''), Surya (''Nitten''), Chandra (''Gatten'').Willem Frederik Stutterheim et al (1995), Rāma-legends and Rāma-reliefs in Indonesia, {{ISBN|978-8170172512}}, pages xiv–xviS Biswas (2000), Art of Japan, Northern, {{ISBN|978-8172112691}}, page 184[[Adrian Snodgrass]] (2007), The Symbolism of the Stupa, Motilal Banarsidass, {{ISBN|978-8120807815}}, pages 120-124, 298-300}}. When the Japanese Empire enforced the ''[[Shinbutsu bunri]]'', the official separation of Shinto shrines and Buddhist temples, shrines celebrating Suiten identified their dedication to [[Amenominakanushi]]."Tokyo Suitengu monogatari" 1985 Kodansha, {{ISBN|406202117X}} Suitengu is located in [[Chūō, Tokyo]]. It is devoted to conception and safe childbirth. In 1818 the ninth [[daimyō]] of the [[Kurume Domain]] established the Suitengu in [[Edo]] as a branch of a shrine of the same name in [[Kurume, Fukuoka]]. It was inside the grounds of the domain's mansion in the Mita district of what is now [[Minato, Tokyo]], and the domain opened it to the public on the fifth day of every month. In 1871, the [[Arima clan|Arima family]] moved from Mita to [[Akasaka, Tokyo|Akasaka]], taking the shrine with them, and in the following year they moved the shrine to its present location, on a site that had been occupied by one of the family's mansions. [[Suitengūmae Station]] is close to this shrine and takes its name from it. There are about twenty-five other shrines of the same name in Japan. ==See also== *[[Kurume Suitengū]] *[[Suijin]] ==Notes== {{reflist|group=note}} ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== *[http://www.suitengu.or.jp/ Official site] (in Japanese) {{Chuo, Tokyo}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Suiten-gu (Tokyo)}} [[Category:1818 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Chūō, Tokyo]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Unranked shrines]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[en:Suitengū (Tokyo)]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[Category:dd]] {{Shinto-stub}}

''Taishakuten''), Agni (''Katen''), Yama (''Emmaten''), Nirrti (''Rasetsuten''), Vayu (''Futen''), Ishana (''Ishanaten''), Kubera (''Tamonten''), Brahma (''Bonten''), Prithvi (''Chiten''), Surya (''Nitten''), Chandra (''Gatten'').Willem Frederik Stutterheim et al (1995), Rāma-legends and Rāma-reliefs in Indonesia, {{ISBN|978-8170172512}}, pages xiv–xviS Biswas (2000), Art of Japan, Northern, {{ISBN|978-8172112691}}, page 184[[Adrian Snodgrass]] (2007), The Symbolism of the Stupa, Motilal Banarsidass, {{ISBN|978-8120807815}}, pages 120-124, 298-300}}. When the Japanese Empire enforced the ''[[Shinbutsu bunri]]'', the official separation of Shinto shrines and Buddhist temples, shrines celebrating Suiten identified their dedication to [[Amenominakanushi]]."Tokyo Suitengu monogatari" 1985 Kodansha, {{ISBN|406202117X}} Suitengu is located in [[Chūō, Tokyo]]. It is devoted to conception and safe childbirth. In 1818 the ninth [[daimyō]] of the [[Kurume Domain]] established the Suitengu in [[Edo]] as a branch of a shrine of the same name in [[Kurume, Fukuoka]]. It was inside the grounds of the domain's mansion in the Mita district of what is now [[Minato, Tokyo]], and the domain opened it to the public on the fifth day of every month. In 1871, the [[Arima clan|Arima family]] moved from Mita to [[Akasaka, Tokyo|Akasaka]], taking the shrine with them, and in the following year they moved the shrine to its present location, on a site that had been occupied by one of the family's mansions. [[Suitengūmae Station]] is close to this shrine and takes its name from it. There are about twenty-five other shrines of the same name in Japan. ==See also== *[[Kurume Suitengū]] *[[Suijin]] ==Notes== {{reflist|group=note}} ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== *[http://www.suitengu.or.jp/ Official site] (in Japanese) {{Chuo, Tokyo}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Suiten-gu (Tokyo)}} [[category:kk]][[category:1818 establishments in Japan]] [[category:kk]][[category:Buildings and structures in Chūō, Tokyo]] [[category:kk]][[category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]] [[category:kk]][[category:Unranked shrines]] [[category:kk]][[category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[category:mm]] [[category:kk]][[category:kk]] [[en:Suitengū (Tokyo)]][[category:kk]][[category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[category:mm]] [[category:kk]][[category:kk]] [[category:kk]][[category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[category:mm]] [[category:kk]][[category:kk]] [[Category:ファイル]] {{Shinto-stub}}

''Taishakuten''), Agni (''Katen''), Yama (''Emmaten''), Nirrti (''Rasetsuten''), Vayu (''Futen''), Ishana (''Ishanaten''), Kubera (''Tamonten''), Brahma (''Bonten''), Prithvi (''Chiten''), Surya (''Nitten''), Chandra (''Gatten'').Willem Frederik Stutterheim et al (1995), Rāma-legends and Rāma-reliefs in Indonesia, {{ISBN|978-8170172512}}, pages xiv–xviS Biswas (2000), Art of Japan, Northern, {{ISBN|978-8172112691}}, page 184[[Adrian Snodgrass]] (2007), The Symbolism of the Stupa, Motilal Banarsidass, {{ISBN|978-8120807815}}, pages 120-124, 298-300}}. When the Japanese Empire enforced the ''[[Shinbutsu bunri]]'', the official separation of Shinto shrines and Buddhist temples, shrines celebrating Suiten identified their dedication to [[Amenominakanushi]]."Tokyo Suitengu monogatari" 1985 Kodansha, {{ISBN|406202117X}} Suitengu is located in [[Chūō, Tokyo]]. It is devoted to conception and safe childbirth. In 1818 the ninth [[daimyō]] of the [[Kurume Domain]] established the Suitengu in [[Edo]] as a branch of a shrine of the same name in [[Kurume, Fukuoka]]. It was inside the grounds of the domain's mansion in the Mita district of what is now [[Minato, Tokyo]], and the domain opened it to the public on the fifth day of every month. In 1871, the [[Arima clan|Arima family]] moved from Mita to [[Akasaka, Tokyo|Akasaka]], taking the shrine with them, and in the following year they moved the shrine to its present location, on a site that had been occupied by one of the family's mansions. [[Suitengūmae Station]] is close to this shrine and takes its name from it. There are about twenty-five other shrines of the same name in Japan. ==See also== *[[Kurume Suitengū]] *[[Suijin]] ==Notes== {{reflist|group=note}} ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== *[http://www.suitengu.or.jp/ Official site] (in Japanese) {{Chuo, Tokyo}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Suiten-gu (Tokyo)}} [[category:kk]][[category:1818 establishments in Japan]] [[category:kk]][[category:Buildings and structures in Chūō, Tokyo]] [[category:kk]][[category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]] [[category:kk]][[category:Unranked shrines]] [[category:kk]][[category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]][[en:Suitengū (Tokyo)]]]] [[category:mm]] [[category:kk]][[category:kk]] [[en:Suitengū (Tokyo)]][[category:kk]][[category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]][[en:Suitengū (Tokyo)]]]] [[category:mm]] [[category:kk]][[category:kk]] [[category:kk]][[category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]][[en:Suitengū (Tokyo)]]]] [[category:mm]] [[category:kk]][[category:kk]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Suitengū (Tokyo)]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:bump1-19]] {{Shinto-stub}}

[[Tokyo]] 103-0014 | website = {{URL|http://www.suitengu.or.jp/}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Suiten-gū'''|水天宮}}, literally "Palace of the Water [[Deva (Buddhism)|Deva]]", or "Palace of Suiten", is a [[Shinto shrine]] dedicated to four deities: * [[Ame-no-Minakanushi|Amenominakanushi]] * [[Emperor Antoku|Antoku]] * [[Taira no Tokuko|Kenrenmon-in]] * [[Taira no Tokiko|Nii No Ama]] "Suiten" is the [[Japan]]ese name of the deity of [[Hindu]] origins [[Varuna]], one of a series of Hindu deities whose worship entered Japan together with [[Buddhism]].{{refn|group=note|Varuna is one among a number of Hindu deities incorporated into [[Shinto]], introduced into Japan together with Buddhism: Indra (Jap. ''Taishakuten''), Agni (''Katen''), Yama (''Emmaten''), Nirrti (''Rasetsuten''), Vayu (''Futen''), Ishana (''Ishanaten''), Kubera (''Tamonten''), Brahma (''Bonten''), Prithvi (''Chiten''), Surya (''Nitten''), Chandra (''Gatten'').Willem Frederik Stutterheim et al (1995), Rāma-legends and Rāma-reliefs in Indonesia, {{ISBN|978-8170172512}}, pages xiv–xviS Biswas (2000), Art of Japan, Northern, {{ISBN|978-8172112691}}, page 184[[Adrian Snodgrass]] (2007), The Symbolism of the Stupa, Motilal Banarsidass, {{ISBN|978-8120807815}}, pages 120-124, 298-300}}. When the Japanese Empire enforced the ''[[Shinbutsu bunri]]'', the official separation of Shinto shrines and Buddhist temples, shrines celebrating Suiten identified their dedication to [[Amenominakanushi]]."Tokyo Suitengu monogatari" 1985 Kodansha, {{ISBN|406202117X}} Suitengu is located in [[Chūō, Tokyo]]. It is devoted to conception and safe childbirth. In 1818 the ninth [[daimyō]] of the [[Kurume Domain]] established the Suitengu in [[Edo]] as a branch of a shrine of the same name in [[Kurume, Fukuoka]]. It was inside the grounds of the domain's mansion in the Mita district of what is now [[Minato, Tokyo]], and the domain opened it to the public on the fifth day of every month. In 1871, the [[Arima clan|Arima family]] moved from Mita to [[Akasaka, Tokyo|Akasaka]], taking the shrine with them, and in the following year they moved the shrine to its present location, on a site that had been occupied by one of the family's mansions. [[Suitengūmae Station]] is close to this shrine and takes its name from it. There are about twenty-five other shrines of the same name in Japan. ==See also== *[[Kurume Suitengū]] *[[Suijin]] ==Notes== {{reflist|group=note}} ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== *[http://www.suitengu.or.jp/ Official site] (in Japanese) {{Chuo, Tokyo}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Suiten-gu (Tokyo)}} [[category:1818 establishments in Japan]] [[category:Buildings and structures in Chūō, Tokyo]] [[category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]] [[category:Unranked shrines]] [[category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]][[en:Suitengū (Tokyo)]]]] [[category:mm]] [[en:Suitengū (Tokyo)]][[category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]][[en:Suitengū (Tokyo)]]]] [[category:mm]] [[category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]][[en:Suitengū (Tokyo)]]]] [[category:mm]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Suitengū (Tokyo)]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] {{Shinto-stub}}

''Taishakuten''), Agni (''Katen''), Yama (''Emmaten''), Nirrti (''Rasetsuten''), Vayu (''Futen''), Ishana (''Ishanaten''), Kubera (''Tamonten''), Brahma (''Bonten''), Prithvi (''Chiten''), Surya (''Nitten''), Chandra (''Gatten'').Willem Frederik Stutterheim et al (1995), Rāma-legends and Rāma-reliefs in Indonesia, {{ISBN|978-8170172512}}, pages xiv–xviS Biswas (2000), Art of Japan, Northern, {{ISBN|978-8172112691}}, page 184[[Adrian Snodgrass]] (2007), The Symbolism of the Stupa, Motilal Banarsidass, {{ISBN|978-8120807815}}, pages 120-124, 298-300}}. When the Japanese Empire enforced the ''[[Shinbutsu bunri]]'', the official separation of Shinto shrines and Buddhist temples, shrines celebrating Suiten identified their dedication to [[Amenominakanushi]]."Tokyo Suitengu monogatari" 1985 Kodansha, {{ISBN|406202117X}} Suitengu is located in [[Chūō, Tokyo]]. It is devoted to conception and safe childbirth. In 1818 the ninth [[daimyō]] of the [[Kurume Domain]] established the Suitengu in [[Edo]] as a branch of a shrine of the same name in [[Kurume, Fukuoka]]. It was inside the grounds of the domain's mansion in the Mita district of what is now [[Minato, Tokyo]], and the domain opened it to the public on the fifth day of every month. In 1871, the [[Arima clan|Arima family]] moved from Mita to [[Akasaka, Tokyo|Akasaka]], taking the shrine with them, and in the following year they moved the shrine to its present location, on a site that had been occupied by one of the family's mansions. [[Suitengūmae Station]] is close to this shrine and takes its name from it. There are about twenty-five other shrines of the same name in Japan. ==See also== *[[Kurume Suitengū]] *[[Suijin]] ==Notes== {{reflist|group=note}} ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== *[http://www.suitengu.or.jp/ Official site] (in Japanese) {{Chuo, Tokyo}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Suiten-gu (Tokyo)}} [[Category:1818 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Chūō, Tokyo]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Unranked shrines]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 1 6]][[en:Suitengū (Tokyo)]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[en:Suitengū (Tokyo)]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 1 6]][[en:Suitengū (Tokyo)]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 1 6]][[en:Suitengū (Tokyo)]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Suitengū (Tokyo)]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:bump 1-26]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Suitengū (Tokyo)}][[Category:qq]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Suitengū (Tokyo)]][[Category:kk]] {{Shinto-stub}}

[[Taira no Tokiko]] | founded_by = | established = 1818 | date_destroyed = | location = 2-4-1 Nihonbashi-Kakigarachō, [[Chūō, Tokyo|Chūō]]
[[Tokyo]] 103-0014 | website = {{URL|http://www.suitengu.or.jp/}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Suiten-gū'''|水天宮}}, literally "Palace of the Water [[Deva (Buddhism)|Deva]]", or "Palace of Suiten", is a [[Shinto shrine]] dedicated to four deities: * [[Ame-no-Minakanushi|Amenominakanushi]] * [[Emperor Antoku|Antoku]] * [[Taira no Tokuko|Kenrenmon-in]] * [[Taira no Tokiko|Nii No Ama]] "Suiten" is the [[Japan]]ese name of the deity of [[Hindu]] origins [[Varuna]], one of a series of Hindu deities whose worship entered Japan together with [[Buddhism]].{{refn|group=note|Varuna is one among a number of Hindu deities incorporated into [[Shinto]], introduced into Japan together with Buddhism: Indra (Jap. ''Taishakuten''), Agni (''Katen''), Yama (''Emmaten''), Nirrti (''Rasetsuten''), Vayu (''Futen''), Ishana (''Ishanaten''), Kubera (''Tamonten''), Brahma (''Bonten''), Prithvi (''Chiten''), Surya (''Nitten''), Chandra (''Gatten'').Willem Frederik Stutterheim et al (1995), Rāma-legends and Rāma-reliefs in Indonesia, {{ISBN|978-8170172512}}, pages xiv–xviS Biswas (2000), Art of Japan, Northern, {{ISBN|978-8172112691}}, page 184[[Adrian Snodgrass]] (2007), The Symbolism of the Stupa, Motilal Banarsidass, {{ISBN|978-8120807815}}, pages 120-124, 298-300}}. When the Japanese Empire enforced the ''[[Shinbutsu bunri]]'', the official separation of Shinto shrines and Buddhist temples, shrines celebrating Suiten identified their dedication to [[Amenominakanushi]]."Tokyo Suitengu monogatari" 1985 Kodansha, {{ISBN|406202117X}} Suitengu is located in [[Chūō, Tokyo]]. It is devoted to conception and safe childbirth. In 1818 the ninth [[daimyō]] of the [[Kurume Domain]] established the Suitengu in [[Edo]] as a branch of a shrine of the same name in [[Kurume, Fukuoka]]. It was inside the grounds of the domain's mansion in the Mita district of what is now [[Minato, Tokyo]], and the domain opened it to the public on the fifth day of every month. In 1871, the [[Arima clan|Arima family]] moved from Mita to [[Akasaka, Tokyo|Akasaka]], taking the shrine with them, and in the following year they moved the shrine to its present location, on a site that had been occupied by one of the family's mansions. [[Suitengūmae Station]] is close to this shrine and takes its name from it. There are about twenty-five other shrines of the same name in Japan. ==See also== *[[Kurume Suitengū]] *[[Suijin]] ==Notes== {{reflist|group=note}} ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== *[http://www.suitengu.or.jp/ Official site] (in Japanese) {{Chuo, Tokyo}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Suiten-gu (Tokyo)}} [[Category:1818 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Chūō, Tokyo]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Unranked shrines]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[en:Suitengū (Tokyo)]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[en:Suitengū (Tokyo)]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[en:Suitengū (Tokyo)]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[en:Suitengū (Tokyo)]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Suitengū (Tokyo)]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:bump 1-26]] [[en:Suitengū (Tokyo)}][[Category:kk]][[en:Suitengū (Tokyo)]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Suitengū (Tokyo)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:bump February 13, 2025 bump]][[en:Suitengū (Tokyo)]][[Category:bump February 13, 2025 bump]] [[en:Suitengū (Tokyo)]][[en:Suitengū (Tokyo)]]{{Shinto-stub}}

[[Tokyo]] 103-0014 | website = {{URL|http://www.suitengu.or.jp/}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Suiten-gū'''|水天宮}}, literally "Palace of the Water [[Deva (Buddhism)|Deva]]", or "Palace of Suiten", is a [[Shinto shrine]] dedicated to four deities: * [[Ame-no-Minakanushi|Amenominakanushi]] * [[Emperor Antoku|Antoku]] * [[Taira no Tokuko|Kenrenmon-in]] * [[Taira no Tokiko|Nii No Ama]] "Suiten" is the [[Japan]]ese name of the deity of [[Hindu]] origins [[Varuna]], one of a series of Hindu deities whose worship entered Japan together with [[Buddhism]].{{refn|group=note|Varuna is one among a number of Hindu deities incorporated into [[Shinto]], introduced into Japan together with Buddhism: Indra (Jap. ''Taishakuten''), Agni (''Katen''), Yama (''Emmaten''), Nirrti (''Rasetsuten''), Vayu (''Futen''), Ishana (''Ishanaten''), Kubera (''Tamonten''), Brahma (''Bonten''), Prithvi (''Chiten''), Surya (''Nitten''), Chandra (''Gatten'').Willem Frederik Stutterheim et al (1995), Rāma-legends and Rāma-reliefs in Indonesia, {{ISBN|978-8170172512}}, pages xiv–xviS Biswas (2000), Art of Japan, Northern, {{ISBN|978-8172112691}}, page 184[[Adrian Snodgrass]] (2007), The Symbolism of the Stupa, Motilal Banarsidass, {{ISBN|978-8120807815}}, pages 120-124, 298-300}}. When the Japanese Empire enforced the ''[[Shinbutsu bunri]]'', the official separation of Shinto shrines and Buddhist temples, shrines celebrating Suiten identified their dedication to [[Amenominakanushi]]."Tokyo Suitengu monogatari" 1985 Kodansha, {{ISBN|406202117X}} Suitengu is located in [[Chūō, Tokyo]]. It is devoted to conception and safe childbirth. In 1818 the ninth [[daimyō]] of the [[Kurume Domain]] established the Suitengu in [[Edo]] as a branch of a shrine of the same name in [[Kurume, Fukuoka]]. It was inside the grounds of the domain's mansion in the Mita district of what is now [[Minato, Tokyo]], and the domain opened it to the public on the fifth day of every month. In 1871, the [[Arima clan|Arima family]] moved from Mita to [[Akasaka, Tokyo|Akasaka]], taking the shrine with them, and in the following year they moved the shrine to its present location, on a site that had been occupied by one of the family's mansions. [[Suitengūmae Station]] is close to this shrine and takes its name from it. There are about twenty-five other shrines of the same name in Japan. ==See also== *[[Kurume Suitengū]] *[[Suijin]] ==Notes== {{reflist|group=note}} ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== *[http://www.suitengu.or.jp/ Official site] (in Japanese) {{Chuo, Tokyo}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Suiten-gu (Tokyo)}} [[Category:1818 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Chūō, Tokyo]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Unranked shrines]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]][[Category:qq 6]] [[en:Suitengū (Tokyo)]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[en:Suitengū (Tokyo)]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]][[Category:qq 6]] [[en:Suitengū (Tokyo)]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]][[Category:qq 6]] [[en:Suitengū (Tokyo)]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:Suitengū (Tokyo)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:bump 1-26]] [[en:Suitengū (Tokyo)]][[Category:kk]][[en:Suitengū (Tokyo)]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Suitengū (Tokyo)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:bump February 13, 2025 bump]][[en:Suitengū (Tokyo)]][[Category:bump February 13, 2025 bump]] [[Category:Suiten-gū Shrines]] [[en:Suitengū (Tokyo)]][[en:Suitengū (Tokyo)]]{{Shinto-stub}} [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Suitengū (Tokyo)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]]

It was inside the grounds of the domain's mansion in the Mita district of what is now [[Minato, Tokyo]], and the domain opened it to the public on the fifth day of every month. In 1871, the [[Arima clan|Arima family]] moved from Mita to [[Akasaka, Tokyo|Akasaka]], taking the shrine with them, and in the following year they moved the shrine to its present location, on a site that had been occupied by one of the family's mansions. [[Suitengūmae Station]] is close to this shrine and takes its name from it. {{Infobox religious building | name = Suiten-gū
水天宮 | image = Tokyo Suitengu 201604a.jpg | caption = The current shrine (Rebuilt in 2016) | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|41|01|N|139|47|06|E|region:JP_scale:30000_source:jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Amenominakanushi]]（as [[Varuna]]）
[[Emperor Antoku]]
[[Taira no Tokuko]]
[[Taira no Tokiko]] | founded_by = | established = 1818 | date_destroyed = | location = 2-4-1 Nihonbashi-Kakigarachō, [[Chūō, Tokyo|Chūō]]
[[Tokyo]] 103-0014 | website = {{URL|http://www.suitengu.or.jp/}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Suiten-gū'''|水天宮}}, literally "Palace of the Water [[Deva (Buddhism)|Deva]]", or "Palace of Suiten", is a [[Shinto shrine]] dedicated to four deities: * [[Ame-no-Minakanushi|Amenominakanushi]] * [[Emperor Antoku|Antoku]] * [[Taira no Tokuko|Kenrenmon-in]] * [[Taira no Tokiko|Nii No Ama]] "Suiten" is the [[Japan]]ese name of the deity of [[Hindu]] origins [[Varuna]], one of a series of Hindu deities whose worship entered Japan together with [[Buddhism]].{{refn|group=note|Varuna is one among a number of Hindu deities incorporated into [[Shinto]], introduced into Japan together with Buddhism: Indra (Jap. ''Taishakuten''), Agni (''Katen''), Yama (''Emmaten''), Nirrti (''Rasetsuten''), Vayu (''Futen''), Ishana (''Ishanaten''), Kubera (''Tamonten''), Brahma (''Bonten''), Prithvi (''Chiten''), Surya (''Nitten''), Chandra (''Gatten'').Willem Frederik Stutterheim et al (1995), Rāma-legends and Rāma-reliefs in Indonesia, {{ISBN|978-8170172512}}, pages xiv–xviS Biswas (2000), Art of Japan, Northern, {{ISBN|978-8172112691}}, page 184[[Adrian Snodgrass]] (2007), The Symbolism of the Stupa, Motilal Banarsidass, {{ISBN|978-8120807815}}, pages 120-124, 298-300}}. When the Japanese Empire enforced the ''[[Shinbutsu bunri]]'', the official separation of Shinto shrines and Buddhist temples, shrines celebrating Suiten identified their dedication to [[Amenominakanushi]]."Tokyo Suitengu monogatari" 1985 Kodansha, {{ISBN|406202117X}} Suitengu is located in [[Chūō, Tokyo]]. It is devoted to conception and safe childbirth. In 1818 the ninth [[daimyō]] of the [[Kurume Domain]] established the Suitengu in [[Edo]] as a branch of a shrine of the same name in [[Kurume, Fukuoka]]. It was inside the grounds of the domain's mansion in the Mita district of what is now [[Minato, Tokyo]], and the domain opened it to the public on the fifth day of every month. In 1871, the [[Arima clan|Arima family]] moved from Mita to [[Akasaka, Tokyo|Akasaka]], taking the shrine with them, and in the following year they moved the shrine to its present location, on a site that had been occupied by one of the family's mansions. [[Suitengūmae Station]] is close to this shrine and takes its name from it. There are about twenty-five other shrines of the same name in Japan. ==See also== *[[Kurume Suitengū]] *[[Suijin]] ==Notes== {{reflist|group=note}} ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== *[http://www.suitengu.or.jp/ Official site] (in Japanese) {{Chuo, Tokyo}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Suiten-gu (Tokyo)}} [[Category:1818 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Chūō, Tokyo]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Unranked shrines]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]][[Category:11 6]] [[en:Suitengū (Tokyo)]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[en:Suitengū (Tokyo)]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]][[Category:11 6]] [[en:Suitengū (Tokyo)]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]][[Category:11 6]] [[en:Suitengū (Tokyo)]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:11]][[Category:Suitengū (Tokyo)]][[Category:11]] [[Category:bump 1-26]] [[en:Suitengū (Tokyo)]][[Category:kk]][[en:Suitengū (Tokyo)]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:11]][[en:Suitengū (Tokyo)]][[Category:11]] [[Category:bump February 13, 2025 bump]][[en:Suitengū (Tokyo)]][[Category:bump February 13, 2025 bump]] [[Category:Suiten-gū Shrines]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Suitengū (Tokyo)]][[Category:11]] [[en:Suitengū (Tokyo)]][[en:Suitengū (Tokyo)]]{{Shinto-stub}} [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Suitengū (Tokyo)]][[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]]

水天宮 | image = Tokyo Suitengu 201604a.jpg | caption = The current shrine (Rebuilt in 2016) | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|41|01|N|139|47|06|E|region:JP_scale:30000_source:jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Amenominakanushi]]（as [[Varuna]]）
[[Emperor Antoku]]
[[Taira no Tokuko]]
[[Taira no Tokiko]] | founded_by = | established = 1818 | date_destroyed = | location = 2-4-1 Nihonbashi-Kakigarachō, [[Chūō, Tokyo|Chūō]]
[[Tokyo]] 103-0014 | website = {{URL|http://www.suitengu.or.jp/}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Suiten-gū'''|水天宮}}, literally "Palace of the Water [[Deva (Buddhism)|Deva]]", or "Palace of Suiten", is a [[Shinto shrine]] dedicated to four deities: * [[Ame-no-Minakanushi|Amenominakanushi]] * [[Emperor Antoku|Antoku]] * [[Taira no Tokuko|Kenrenmon-in]] * [[Taira no Tokiko|Nii No Ama]] "Suiten" is the [[Japan]]ese name of the deity of [[Hindu]] origins [[Varuna]], one of a series of Hindu deities whose worship entered Japan together with [[Buddhism]].{{refn|group=note|Varuna is one among a number of Hindu deities incorporated into [[Shinto]], introduced into Japan together with Buddhism: Indra (Jap. ''Taishakuten''), Agni (''Katen''), Yama (''Emmaten''), Nirrti (''Rasetsuten''), Vayu (''Futen''), Ishana (''Ishanaten''), Kubera (''Tamonten''), Brahma (''Bonten''), Prithvi (''Chiten''), Surya (''Nitten''), Chandra (''Gatten'').Willem Frederik Stutterheim et al (1995), Rāma-legends and Rāma-reliefs in Indonesia, {{ISBN|978-8170172512}}, pages xiv–xviS Biswas (2000), Art of Japan, Northern, {{ISBN|978-8172112691}}, page 184[[Adrian Snodgrass]] (2007), The Symbolism of the Stupa, Motilal Banarsidass, {{ISBN|978-8120807815}}, pages 120-124, 298-300}}. When the Japanese Empire enforced the ''[[Shinbutsu bunri]]'', the official separation of Shinto shrines and Buddhist temples, shrines celebrating Suiten identified their dedication to [[Amenominakanushi]]."Tokyo Suitengu monogatari" 1985 Kodansha, {{ISBN|406202117X}} Suitengu is located in [[Chūō, Tokyo]]. It is devoted to conception and safe childbirth. In 1818 the ninth [[daimyō]] of the [[Kurume Domain]] established the Suitengu in [[Edo]] as a branch of a shrine of the same name in [[Kurume, Fukuoka]]. It was inside the grounds of the domain's mansion in the Mita district of what is now [[Minato, Tokyo]], and the domain opened it to the public on the fifth day of every month. In 1871, the [[Arima clan|Arima family]] moved from Mita to [[Akasaka, Tokyo|Akasaka]], taking the shrine with them, and in the following year they moved the shrine to its present location, on a site that had been occupied by one of the family's mansions. [[Suitengūmae Station]] is close to this shrine and takes its name from it. There are about twenty-five other shrines of the same name in Japan. ==See also== *[[Kurume Suitengū]] *[[Suijin]] ==Notes== {{reflist|group=note}} ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== *[http://www.suitengu.or.jp/ Official site] (in Japanese) {{Chuo, Tokyo}} {{Shinto shrine}} ]] ]] ]] {{Shinto-stub}} {{draft categories| [[Category:1818 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Chūō, Tokyo]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Unranked shrines]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:11 6]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Suitengū (Tokyo)]] [[Category:bump 1-26]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Suiten-gū Shrines]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] }} [[sn:Suitengū (Tokyo)]] [[en:Suitengū (Tokyo)]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Suiten-gu (Tokyo)}}

''Taishakuten''), Agni (''Katen''), Yama (''Emmaten''), Nirrti (''Rasetsuten''), Vayu (''Futen''), Ishana (''Ishanaten''), Kubera (''Tamonten''), Brahma (''Bonten''), Prithvi (''Chiten''), Surya (''Nitten''), Chandra (''Gatten'').Willem Frederik Stutterheim et al (1995), Rāma-legends and Rāma-reliefs in Indonesia, {{ISBN|978-8170172512}}, pages xiv–xviS Biswas (2000), Art of Japan, Northern, {{ISBN|978-8172112691}}, page 184[[Adrian Snodgrass]] (2007), The Symbolism of the Stupa, Motilal Banarsidass, {{ISBN|978-8120807815}}, pages 120-124, 298-300}}. When the Japanese Empire enforced the ''[[Shinbutsu bunri]]'', the official separation of Shinto shrines and Buddhist temples, shrines celebrating Suiten identified their dedication to [[Amenominakanushi]]."Tokyo Suitengu monogatari" 1985 Kodansha, {{ISBN|406202117X}} Suitengu is located in [[Chūō, Tokyo]]. It is devoted to conception and safe childbirth. In 1818 the ninth [[daimyō]] of the [[Kurume Domain]] established the Suitengu in [[Edo]] as a branch of a shrine of the same name in [[Kurume, Fukuoka]]. It was inside the grounds of the domain's mansion in the Mita district of what is now [[Minato, Tokyo]], and the domain opened it to the public on the fifth day of every month. In 1871, the [[Arima clan|Arima family]] moved from Mita to [[Akasaka, Tokyo|Akasaka]], taking the shrine with them, and in the following year they moved the shrine to its present location, on a site that had been occupied by one of the family's mansions. [[Suitengūmae Station]] is close to this shrine and takes its name from it. There are about twenty-five other shrines of the same name in Japan. ==See also== *[[Kurume Suitengū]] *[[Suijin]] ==Notes== {{reflist|group=note}} ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== *[http://www.suitengu.or.jp/ Official site] (in Japanese) {{Chuo, Tokyo}} {{Shinto shrine}}]] {{Shinto-stub}} {{draft categories| [[Category:1818 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Chūō, Tokyo]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Unranked shrines]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Suitengū (Tokyo)]] [[Category:Suiten-gū Shrines]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] }} [[sn:Suitengū (Tokyo)]] [[en:Suitengū (Tokyo)]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Suiten-gu (Tokyo)}}

In 1871, the [[Arima clan|Arima family]] moved from Mita to [[Akasaka, Tokyo|Akasaka]], taking the shrine with them, and in the following year they moved the shrine to its present location, on a site that had been occupied by one of the family's mansions. [[Suitengūmae Station]] is close to this shrine and takes its name from it. There are about twenty-five other shrines of the same name in Japan. ==See also== *[[Kurume Suitengū]] *[[Suijin]] ==Notes== {{reflist|group=note}} ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== *[http://www.suitengu.or.jp/ Official site] (in Japanese) {{Chuo, Tokyo}} {{Shinto shrine}}]] {{Shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Suiten-gu (Tokyo)}} {{draft categories| [[Category:1818 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Chūō, Tokyo]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:UnRanked shrines]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Suitengū (Tokyo)]] [[Category:Suiten-gū Shrines]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] }} [[sn:Suitengū (Tokyo)]] [[en:Suitengū (Tokyo)]]

[[Emperor Antoku]]
[[Taira no Tokuko]]
[[Taira no Tokiko]] | founded_by = | established = 1818 | date_destroyed = | location = 2-4-1 Nihonbashi-Kakigarachō, [[Chūō, Tokyo|Chūō]]
[[Tokyo]] 103-0014 | website = {{URL|http://www.suitengu.or.jp/}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Suiten-gū'''|水天宮}}, literally "Palace of the Water [[Deva (Buddhism)|Deva]]", or "Palace of Suiten", is a [[Shinto shrine]] dedicated to four deities: * [[Ame-no-Minakanushi|Amenominakanushi]] * [[Emperor Antoku|Antoku]] * [[Taira no Tokuko|Kenrenmon-in]] * [[Taira no Tokiko|Nii No Ama]] "Suiten" is the [[Japan]]ese name of the deity of [[Hindu]] origins [[Varuna]], one of a series of Hindu deities whose worship entered Japan together with [[Buddhism]].{{refn|group=note|Varuna is one among a number of Hindu deities incorporated into [[Shinto]], introduced into Japan together with Buddhism: Indra (Jap. ''Taishakuten''), Agni (''Katen''), Yama (''Emmaten''), Nirrti (''Rasetsuten''), Vayu (''Futen''), Ishana (''Ishanaten''), Kubera (''Tamonten''), Brahma (''Bonten''), Prithvi (''Chiten''), Surya (''Nitten''), Chandra (''Gatten'').Willem Frederik Stutterheim et al (1995), Rāma-legends and Rāma-reliefs in Indonesia, {{ISBN|978-8170172512}}, pages xiv–xviS Biswas (2000), Art of Japan, Northern, {{ISBN|978-8172112691}}, page 184[[Adrian Snodgrass]] (2007), The Symbolism of the Stupa, Motilal Banarsidass, {{ISBN|978-8120807815}}, pages 120-124, 298-300}}. When the Japanese Empire enforced the ''[[Shinbutsu bunri]]'', the official separation of Shinto shrines and Buddhist temples, shrines celebrating Suiten identified their dedication to [[Amenominakanushi]]."Tokyo Suitengu monogatari" 1985 Kodansha, {{ISBN|406202117X}} Suitengu is located in [[Chūō, Tokyo]]. It is devoted to conception and safe childbirth. In 1818 the ninth [[daimyō]] of the [[Kurume Domain]] established the Suitengu in [[Edo]] as a branch of a shrine of the same name in [[Kurume, Fukuoka]]. In 1871, the [[Arima clan|Arima family]] moved from Mita to [[Akasaka, Tokyo|Akasaka]], taking the shrine with them, and in the following year they moved the shrine to its present location, on a site that had been occupied by one of the family's mansions. [[Suitengūmae Station]] is close to this shrine and takes its name from it. There are about twenty-five other shrines of the same name in Japan. ==See also== *[[Kurume Suitengū]] *[[Suijin]] ==Notes== {{reflist|group=note}} ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== *[http://www.suitengu.or.jp/ Official site] (in Japanese) {{Chuo, Tokyo}} {{Shinto shrine}}]] {{Shinto-stub}} {{draft categories| [[Category:1818 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Chūō, Tokyo]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:UnRanked shrines]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Suitengū (Tokyo)]] [[Category:Suiten-gū Shrines]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] }} [[sn:Suitengū (Tokyo)]] [[en:Suitengū (Tokyo)]]

''Taishakuten''), Agni (''Katen''), Yama (''Emmaten''), Nirrti (''Rasetsuten''), Vayu (''Futen''), Ishana (''Ishanaten''), Kubera (''Tamonten''), Brahma (''Bonten''), Prithvi (''Chiten''), Surya (''Nitten''), Chandra (''Gatten'').Willem Frederik Stutterheim et al (1995), Rāma-legends and Rāma-reliefs in Indonesia, {{ISBN|978-8170172512}}, pages xiv–xviS Biswas (2000), Art of Japan, Northern, {{ISBN|978-8172112691}}, page 184[[Adrian Snodgrass]] (2007), The Symbolism of the Stupa, Motilal Banarsidass, {{ISBN|978-8120807815}}, pages 120-124, 298-300}}. When the Japanese Empire enforced the ''[[Shinbutsu bunri]]'', the official separation of Shinto shrines and Buddhist temples, shrines celebrating Suiten identified their dedication to [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]]."Tokyo Suitengu monogatari" 1985 Kodansha, {{ISBN|406202117X}} Suitengu is located in [[Chūō, Tokyo]]. It is devoted to conception and safe childbirth. In 1818 the ninth [[daimyō]] of the [[Kurume Domain]] established the Suitengu in [[Edo]] as a branch of a shrine of the same name in [[Kurume, Fukuoka]]. It was inside the grounds of the domain's mansion in the Mita district of what is now [[Minato, Tokyo]], and the domain opened it to the public on the fifth day of every month. In 1871, the [[Arima clan|Arima family]] moved from Mita to [[Akasaka, Tokyo|Akasaka]], taking the shrine with them, and in the following year they moved the shrine to its present location, on a site that had been occupied by one of the family's mansions. [[Suitengūmae Station]] is close to this shrine and takes its name from it. There are about twenty-five other shrines of the same name in Japan. ==See also== *[[Kurume Suitengū]] *[[Suijin]] ==Notes== {{reflist|group=note}} ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== *[http://www.suitengu.or.jp/ Official site] (in Japanese) {{Chuo, Tokyo}} {{Shinto shrine}}]] {{Shinto-stub}} {{draft categories| [[Category:1818 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Chūō, Tokyo]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Unranked shrines]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[Category:Suiten-gū Shrines]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] }} [[sn:Suitengū (Tokyo)]] [[en:Suitengū (Tokyo)]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]]

''Taishakuten''), Agni (''Katen''), Yama (''Emmaten''), Nirrti (''Rasetsuten''), Vayu (''Futen''), Ishana (''Ishanaten''), Kubera (''Tamonten''), Brahma (''Bonten''), Prithvi (''Chiten''), Surya (''Nitten''), Chandra (''Gatten'').Willem Frederik Stutterheim et al (1995), Rāma-legends and Rāma-reliefs in Indonesia, {{ISBN|978-8170172512}}, pages xiv–xviS Biswas (2000), Art of Japan, Northern, {{ISBN|978-8172112691}}, page 184[[:en:Adrian Snodgrass|Adrian Snodgrass]] (2007), The Symbolism of the Stupa, Motilal Banarsidass, {{ISBN|978-8120807815}}, pages 120-124, 298-300}}. When the Japanese Empire enforced the ''[[Shinbutsu bunri]]'', the official separation of Shinto shrines and Buddhist temples, shrines celebrating Suiten identified their dedication to [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]]."Tokyo Suitengu monogatari" 1985 Kodansha, {{ISBN|406202117X}} Suitengu is located in [[Chūō, Tokyo]]. It is devoted to conception and safe childbirth. In 1818 the ninth [[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]] of the [[Kurume Domain]] established the Suitengu in [[Edo]] as a branch of a shrine of the same name in [[Kurume, Fukuoka]]. It was inside the grounds of the domain's mansion in the Mita district of what is now [[Minato, Tokyo]], and the domain opened it to the public on the fifth day of every month. In 1871, the [[:en:Arima clan|Arima family]] moved from Mita to [[:en:Akasaka, Tokyo|Akasaka]], taking the shrine with them, and in the following year they moved the shrine to its present location, on a site that had been occupied by one of the family's mansions. [[Suitengūmae Station]] is close to this shrine and takes its name from it. There are about twenty-five other shrines of the same name in Japan. ==See also== *[[Kurume Suitengū]] *[[Suijin]] ==Notes== {{reflist|group=note}} ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== *[http://www.suitengu.or.jp/ Official site] (in Japanese) {{Chuo, Tokyo}} {{Shinto shrine}}]] {{Shinto-stub}} {{draft categories| [[Category:1818 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Chūō, Tokyo]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Unranked shrines]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[Category:Suiten-gū Shrines]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] }} [[sn:Suitengū (Tokyo)]] [[en:Suitengū (Tokyo)]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:pages with valid en interwikis]] {{Commons category|Suiten-gū (Chuo, Tokyo)}} [[de:Suiten-gū (Tokio)]] [[fr:Suiten-gū (Tokyo)]] [[ja:水天宮 (東京都中央区)]] [[th:ศาลเจ้าซุยเต็น (โตเกียว)]] [[zh:水天宮 (東京都中央區)]]

[[Taira no Tokiko]] | founded_by = | established = 1818 | date_destroyed = | location = 2-4-1 Nihonbashi-Kakigarachō, [[Chūō, Tokyo|Chūō]]
[[Tokyo]] 103-0014 | website = {{URL|http://www.suitengu.or.jp/}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Suiten-gū'''|水天宮}}, literally "Palace of the Water [[:en:Deva (Buddhism)|Deva]]", or "Palace of Suiten", is a [[Shinto shrine]] dedicated to four deities: * [[Ame-no-Minakanushi|Amenominakanushi]] * [[:en:Emperor Antoku|Antoku]] * [[Taira no Tokuko|Kenrenmon-in]] * [[Taira no Tokiko|Nii No Ama]] "Suiten" is the [[Japan]]ese name of the deity of [[Hindu]] origins [[Varuna]], one of a series of Hindu deities whose worship entered Japan together with [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]].{{refn|group=note|Varuna is one among a number of Hindu deities incorporated into [[Shinto]], introduced into Japan together with Buddhism: Indra (Jap. ''Taishakuten''), Agni (''Katen''), Yama (''Emmaten''), Nirrti (''Rasetsuten''), Vayu (''Futen''), Ishana (''Ishanaten''), Kubera (''Tamonten''), Brahma (''Bonten''), Prithvi (''Chiten''), Surya (''Nitten''), Chandra (''Gatten'').Willem Frederik Stutterheim et al (1995), Rāma-legends and Rāma-reliefs in Indonesia, {{ISBN|978-8170172512}}, pages xiv–xviS Biswas (2000), Art of Japan, Northern, {{ISBN|978-8172112691}}, page 184[[:en:Adrian Snodgrass|Adrian Snodgrass]] (2007), The Symbolism of the Stupa, Motilal Banarsidass, {{ISBN|978-8120807815}}, pages 120-124, 298-300}}. When the Japanese Empire enforced the ''[[Shinbutsu bunri]]'', the official separation of Shinto shrines and Buddhist temples, shrines celebrating Suiten identified their dedication to [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]]."Tokyo Suitengu monogatari" 1985 Kodansha, {{ISBN|406202117X}} Suitengu is located in [[Chūō, Tokyo]]. It is devoted to conception and safe childbirth. In 1818 the ninth [[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]] of the [[Kurume Domain]] established the Suitengu in [[:en:Edo|Edo]] as a branch of a shrine of the same name in [[Kurume, Fukuoka]]. It was inside the grounds of the domain's mansion in the Mita district of what is now [[Minato, Tokyo]], and the domain opened it to the public on the fifth day of every month. In 1871, the [[:en:Arima clan|Arima family]] moved from Mita to [[:en:Akasaka, Tokyo|Akasaka]], taking the shrine with them, and in the following year they moved the shrine to its present location, on a site that had been occupied by one of the family's mansions. [[Suitengūmae Station]] is close to this shrine and takes its name from it. There are about twenty-five other shrines of the same name in Japan. ==See also== *[[Kurume Suitengū]] *[[Suijin]] ==Notes== {{reflist|group=note}} ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== *[http://www.suitengu.or.jp/ Official site] (in Japanese) {{Chuo, Tokyo}} {{Shinto shrine}}]] {{Shinto-stub}} {{draft categories| [[Category:1818 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Chūō, Tokyo]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Unranked shrines]] [[Category:Suiten-gū Shrines]] }} [[sn:Suitengū (Tokyo)]] [[en:Suitengū (Tokyo)]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Pages with valid en interwikis]] [[Category:pages with valid en interwikis 2]] {{Commons category|Suiten-gū (Chuo, Tokyo)}} [[de:Suiten-gū (Tokio)]] [[fr:Suiten-gū (Tokyo)]] [[ja:水天宮 (東京都中央区)]] [[th:ศาลเจ้าซุยเต็น (โตเกียว)]] [[zh:水天宮 (東京都中央區)]]

When the Japanese Empire enforced the ''[[Shinbutsu bunri]]'', the official separation of Shinto shrines and Buddhist temples, shrines celebrating Suiten identified their dedication to [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]]."Tokyo Suitengu monogatari" 1985 Kodansha, {{ISBN|406202117X}} Suitengu is located in [[:en:Chūō, Tokyo|Chūō, Tokyo]]. It is devoted to conception and safe childbirth. In 1818 the ninth [[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]] of the [[:en:Kurume Domain|Kurume Domain]] established the Suitengu in [[:en:Edo|Edo]] as a branch of a shrine of the same name in [[:en:Kurume|Kurume]]. It was inside the grounds of the domain's mansion in the Mita district of what is now [[:en:Minato, Tokyo|Minato, Tokyo]], and the domain opened it to the public on the fifth day of every month. In 1871, the [[:en:Arima clan|Arima family]] moved from Mita to [[:en:Akasaka, Tokyo|Akasaka]], taking the shrine with them, and in the following year they moved the shrine to its present location, on a site that had been occupied by one of the family's mansions. [[:en:Suitengūmae Station|Suitengūmae Station]] is close to this shrine and takes its name from it. There are about twenty-five other shrines of the same name in Japan. ==See also== *[[Kurume Suitengū]] *[[Suijin]] ==Notes== {{reflist|group=note}} ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== *[http://www.suitengu.or.jp/ Official site] (in Japanese) {{Chuo, Tokyo}} {{Shinto shrine}}]] {{Shinto-stub}} {{draft categories| [[Category:1818 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Chūō, Tokyo]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Unranked shrines]] [[Category:Suiten-gū Shrines]] }} [[sn:Suitengū (Tokyo)]] [[en:Suitengū (Tokyo)]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Suiten-gū (Chuo, Tokyo)}} [[de:Suiten-gū (Tokio)]] [[fr:Suiten-gū (Tokyo)]] [[ja:水天宮 (東京都中央区)]] [[th:ศาลเจ้าซุยเต็น (โตเกียว)]] [[zh:水天宮 (東京都中央區)]]

In 1871, the [[:en:Arima clan|Arima family]] moved from Mita to [[:en:Akasaka, Tokyo|Akasaka]], taking the shrine with them, and in the following year they moved the shrine to its present location, on a site that had been occupied by one of the family's mansions. [[:en:Suitengūmae Station|Suitengūmae Station]] is close to this shrine and takes its name from it. There are about twenty-five other shrines of the same name in Japan. ==See also== *[[Kurume Suitengū]] *[[Suijin]] ==Notes== {{reflist|group=note}} ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== *[http://www.suitengu.or.jp/ Official site] (in Japanese) {{Chuo, Tokyo}} ]] {{Shinto-stub}} {{draft categories| [[Category:1818 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Chūō, Tokyo]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Unranked shrines]] [[Category:Suiten-gū Shrines]] }} [[sn:Suitengū (Tokyo)]] [[en:Suitengū (Tokyo)]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Suiten-gū (Chuo, Tokyo)}} [[de:Suiten-gū (Tokio)]] [[fr:Suiten-gū (Tokyo)]] [[ja:水天宮 (東京都中央区)]] [[th:ศาลเจ้าซุยเต็น (โตเกียว)]] [[zh:水天宮 (東京都中央區)]] {{wikidata link|Q3503320}}

[[:en:Emperor Antoku|Emperor Antoku]]
[[Taira no Tokuko]]
[[:en:Taira no Tokiko|Taira no Tokiko]] | founded_by = | established = 1818 | date_destroyed = | location = 2-4-1 Nihonbashi-Kakigarachō, [[:en:Chūō, Tokyo|Chūō]]
[[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]] 103-0014 | website = {{URL|http://www.suitengu.or.jp/}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Suiten-gū'''|水天宮}}, literally "Palace of the Water [[:en:Deva (Buddhism)|Deva]]", or "Palace of Suiten", is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] dedicated to four deities: * [[Ame-no-Minakanushi|Amenominakanushi]] * [[:en:Emperor Antoku|Antoku]] * [[Taira no Tokuko|Kenrenmon-in]] * [[:en:Taira no Tokiko|Nii No Ama]] "Suiten" is the [[:en:Japan|Japan]]ese name of the deity of [[:en:Hindus|Hindus]] origins [[:en:Varuna|Varuna]], one of a series of Hindu deities whose worship entered Japan together with [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]].{{refn|group=note|Varuna is one among a number of Hindu deities incorporated into [[Shinto]], introduced into Japan together with Buddhism: Indra (Jap. ''Taishakuten''), Agni (''Katen''), Yama (''Emmaten''), Nirrti (''Rasetsuten''), Vayu (''Futen''), Ishana (''Ishanaten''), Kubera (''Tamonten''), Brahma (''Bonten''), Prithvi (''Chiten''), Surya (''Nitten''), Chandra (''Gatten'').Willem Frederik Stutterheim et al (1995), Rāma-legends and Rāma-reliefs in Indonesia, {{ISBN|978-8170172512}}, pages xiv–xviS Biswas (2000), Art of Japan, Northern, {{ISBN|978-8172112691}}, page 184[[:en:Adrian Snodgrass|Adrian Snodgrass]] (2007), The Symbolism of the Stupa, Motilal Banarsidass, {{ISBN|978-8120807815}}, pages 120-124, 298-300}}. When the Japanese Empire enforced the ''[[Shinbutsu bunri]]'', the official separation of Shinto shrines and Buddhist temples, shrines celebrating Suiten identified their dedication to [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]]."Tokyo Suitengu monogatari" 1985 Kodansha, {{ISBN|406202117X}} Suitengu is located in [[:en:Chūō, Tokyo|Chūō, Tokyo]]. It is devoted to conception and safe childbirth. In 1818 the ninth [[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]] of the [[:en:Kurume Domain|Kurume Domain]] established the Suitengu in [[:en:Edo|Edo]] as a branch of a shrine of the same name in [[:en:Kurume|Kurume]]. It was inside the grounds of the domain's mansion in the Mita district of what is now [[:en:Minato, Tokyo|Minato, Tokyo]], and the domain opened it to the public on the fifth day of every month. In 1871, the [[:en:Arima clan|Arima family]] moved from Mita to [[:en:Akasaka, Tokyo|Akasaka]], taking the shrine with them, and in the following year they moved the shrine to its present location, on a site that had been occupied by one of the family's mansions. [[:en:Suitengūmae Station|Suitengūmae Station]] is close to this shrine and takes its name from it. There are about twenty-five other shrines of the same name in Japan. ==See also== *[[Kurume Suitengū]] *[[Suijin]] ==Notes== {{reflist|group=note}} ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== *[http://www.suitengu.or.jp/ Official site] (in Japanese) {{Chuo, Tokyo}} ]] {{Shinto-stub}} {{draft categories| [[Category:1818 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Chūō, Tokyo]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Unranked shrines]] [[Category:Suiten-gū Shrines]] }} [[en:Suitengū (Tokyo)]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Suiten-gū (Chuo, Tokyo)}} [[de:Suiten-gū (Tokio)]] [[fr:Suiten-gū (Tokyo)]] [[ja:水天宮 (東京都中央区)]] [[th:ศาลเจ้าซุยเต็น (โตเกียว)]] [[zh:水天宮 (東京都中央區)]] {{wikidata link|Q3503320}}

[[:en:Suitengūmae Station|Suitengūmae Station]] is close to this shrine and takes its name from it. There are about twenty-five other shrines of the same name in Japan. ==See also== *[[Kurume Suitengū]] *[[Suijin]] ==Notes== {{reflist|group=note}} ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== *[http://www.suitengu.or.jp/ Official site] (in Japanese) {{Chuo, Tokyo}} ]] {{Shinto-stub}} {{draft categories| [[Category:1818 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Chūō, Tokyo]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Unranked shrines]] [[Category:Suiten-gū Shrines]] }} [[en:Suitengū (Tokyo)]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Suiten-gū (Chuo, Tokyo)}} [[de:Suiten-gū (Tokio)]] [[fr:Suiten-gū (Tokyo)]] [[ja:水天宮 (東京都中央区)]] [[th:ศาลเจ้าซุยเต็น (โตเกียว)]] [[zh:水天宮 (東京都中央區)]] {{wikidata link|Q3503320}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]
