水天宮 | image = Tokyo Suitengu 201604a.jpg | caption = The current shrine (Rebuilt in 2016) | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|41|01|N|139|47|06|E|region:JP_scale:30000_source:jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]]（as [[:en:Varuna|Varuna]]）
[[:en:Emperor Antoku|Emperor Antoku]]
[[Taira no Tokuko]]
[[:en:Taira no Tokiko|Taira no Tokiko]] | founded_by = | established = 1818 | date_destroyed = | location = 2-4-1 Nihonbashi-Kakigarachō, [[:en:Chūō, Tokyo|Chūō]]
[[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]] 103-0014 | website = {{URL|http://www.suitengu.or.jp/}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Suiten-gū'''|水天宮}}, literally "Palace of the Water [[:en:Deva (Buddhism)|Deva]]", or "Palace of Suiten", is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] dedicated to four deities: * [[Ame-no-Minakanushi|Amenominakanushi]] * [[:en:Emperor Antoku|Antoku]] * [[Taira no Tokuko|Kenrenmon-in]] * [[:en:Taira no Tokiko|Nii No Ama]] "Suiten" is the [[:en:Japan|Japan]]ese name of the deity of [[:en:Hindus|Hindus]] origins [[:en:Varuna|Varuna]], one of a series of Hindu deities whose worship entered Japan together with [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]].{{refn|group=note|Varuna is one among a number of Hindu deities incorporated into [[Shinto]], introduced into Japan together with Buddhism: Indra (Jap. ''Taishakuten''), Agni (''Katen''), Yama (''Emmaten''), Nirrti (''Rasetsuten''), Vayu (''Futen''), Ishana (''Ishanaten''), Kubera (''Tamonten''), Brahma (''Bonten''), Prithvi (''Chiten''), Surya (''Nitten''), Chandra (''Gatten'').Willem Frederik Stutterheim et al (1995), Rāma-legends and Rāma-reliefs in Indonesia, {{ISBN|978-8170172512}}, pages xiv–xviS Biswas (2000), Art of Japan, Northern, {{ISBN|978-8172112691}}, page 184[[:en:Adrian Snodgrass|Adrian Snodgrass]] (2007), The Symbolism of the Stupa, Motilal Banarsidass, {{ISBN|978-8120807815}}, pages 120-124, 298-300}}. When the Japanese Empire enforced the ''[[Shinbutsu bunri]]'', the official separation of Shinto shrines and Buddhist temples, shrines celebrating Suiten identified their dedication to [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]]."Tokyo Suitengu monogatari" 1985 Kodansha, {{ISBN|406202117X}} Suitengu is located in [[:en:Chūō, Tokyo|Chūō, Tokyo]]. It is devoted to conception and safe childbirth. In 1818 the ninth [[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]] of the [[:en:Kurume Domain|Kurume Domain]] established the Suitengu in [[:en:Edo|Edo]] as a branch of a shrine of the same name in [[:en:Kurume|Kurume]]. It was inside the grounds of the domain's mansion in the Mita district of what is now [[:en:Minato, Tokyo|Minato, Tokyo]], and the domain opened it to the public on the fifth day of every month. In 1871, the [[:en:Arima clan|Arima family]] moved from Mita to [[:en:Akasaka, Tokyo|Akasaka]], taking the shrine with them, and in the following year they moved the shrine to its present location, on a site that had been occupied by one of the family's mansions. [[:en:Suitengūmae Station|Suitengūmae Station]] is close to this shrine and takes its name from it. There are about twenty-five other shrines of the same name in Japan. ==See also== *[[Kurume Suitengū]] *[[Suijin]] ==Notes== {{reflist|group=note}} ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== *[http://www.suitengu.or.jp/ Official site] (in Japanese) {{Chuo, Tokyo}} ]] {{Shinto-stub}} {{draft categories| [[Category:1818 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Chūō, Tokyo]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Unranked shrines]] [[Category:Suiten-gū Shrines]] }} [[en:Suitengū (Tokyo)]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Suiten-gū (Chuo, Tokyo)}} [[de:Suiten-gū (Tokio)]] [[fr:Suiten-gū (Tokyo)]] [[ja:水天宮 (東京都中央区)]] [[th:ศาลเจ้าซุยเต็น (โตเกียว)]] [[zh:水天宮 (東京都中央區)]] {{wikidata link|Q3503320}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]]

''Taishakuten''), Agni (''Katen''), Yama (''Emmaten''), Nirrti (''Rasetsuten''), Vayu (''Futen''), Ishana (''Ishanaten''), Kubera (''Tamonten''), Brahma (''Bonten''), Prithvi (''Chiten''), Surya (''Nitten''), Chandra (''Gatten'').Willem Frederik Stutterheim et al (1995), Rāma-legends and Rāma-reliefs in Indonesia, {{ISBN|978-8170172512}}, pages xiv–xviS Biswas (2000), Art of Japan, Northern, {{ISBN|978-8172112691}}, page 184[[:en:Adrian Snodgrass|Adrian Snodgrass]] (2007), The Symbolism of the Stupa, Motilal Banarsidass, {{ISBN|978-8120807815}}, pages 120-124, 298-300}}. When the Japanese Empire enforced the ''[[Shinbutsu bunri]]'', the official separation of Shinto shrines and Buddhist temples, shrines celebrating Suiten identified their dedication to [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]]."Tokyo Suitengu monogatari" 1985 Kodansha, {{ISBN|406202117X}} Suitengu is located in [[:en:Chūō, Tokyo|Chūō, Tokyo]]. It is devoted to conception and safe childbirth. In 1818 the ninth [[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]] of the [[:en:Kurume Domain|Kurume Domain]] established the Suitengu in [[:en:Edo|Edo]] as a branch of a shrine of the same name in [[:en:Kurume|Kurume]]. It was inside the grounds of the domain's mansion in the Mita district of what is now [[:en:Minato, Tokyo|Minato, Tokyo]], and the domain opened it to the public on the fifth day of every month. In 1871, the [[:en:Arima clan|Arima family]] moved from Mita to [[:en:Akasaka, Tokyo|Akasaka]], taking the shrine with them, and in the following year they moved the shrine to its present location, on a site that had been occupied by one of the family's mansions. [[:en:Suitengūmae Station|Suitengūmae Station]] is close to this shrine and takes its name from it. There are about twenty-five other shrines of the same name in Japan. ==See also== *[[Kurume Suitengū]] *[[Suijin]] ==Notes== {{reflist|group=note}} ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== *[http://www.suitengu.or.jp/ Official site] (in Japanese) {{Chuo, Tokyo}} ]] {{Shinto-stub}} {{draft categories| [[Category:1818 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Chūō, Tokyo]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Unranked shrines]] [[Category:Suiten-gū Shrines]] }} [[en:Suitengū (Tokyo)]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Suiten-gū (Chuo, Tokyo)}} [[de:Suiten-gū (Tokio)]] [[fr:Suiten-gū (Tokyo)]] [[ja:水天宮 (東京都中央区)]] [[th:ศาลเจ้าซุยเต็น (โตเกียว)]] [[zh:水天宮 (東京都中央區)]] {{wikidata link|Q3503320}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] {{wikidata link|Q3503320}} [[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

There are about twenty-five other shrines of the same name in Japan. ==See also== *[[Kurume Suitengū]] *[[Suijin]] ==Notes== {{reflist|group=note}} ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== *[http://www.suitengu.or.jp/ Official site] (in Japanese) {{Chuo, Tokyo}} ]] {{Shinto-stub}} {{draft categories| [[Category:1818 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Chūō, Tokyo]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Unranked shrines]] [[Category:Suiten-gū Shrines]] }} [[en:Suitengū (Tokyo)]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Suiten-gū (Chuo, Tokyo)}} [[de:Suiten-gū (Tokio)]] [[fr:Suiten-gū (Tokyo)]] [[ja:水天宮 (東京都中央区)]] [[th:ศาลเจ้าซุยเต็น (โตเกียว)]] [[zh:水天宮 (東京都中央區)]] {{wikidata link|Q3503320}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] {{wikidata link|Q3503320}} [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]]

水天宮 | image = Tokyo Suitengu 201604a.jpg | caption = The current shrine (Rebuilt in 2016) | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|41|01|N|139|47|06|E|region:JP_scale:30000_source:jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]]（as [[:en:Varuna|Varuna]]）
[[:en:Emperor Antoku|Emperor Antoku]]
[[Taira no Tokuko]]
[[:en:Taira no Tokiko|Taira no Tokiko]] | founded_by = | established = 1818 | date_destroyed = | location = 2-4-1 Nihonbashi-Kakigarachō, [[:en:Chūō, Tokyo|Chūō]]
[[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]] 103-0014 | website = {{URL|http://www.suitengu.or.jp/}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Suiten-gū'''|水天宮}}, literally "Palace of the Water [[:en:Deva (Buddhism)|Deva]]", or "Palace of Suiten", is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] dedicated to four deities: * [[Ame-no-Minakanushi|Amenominakanushi]] * [[:en:Emperor Antoku|Antoku]] * [[Taira no Tokuko|Kenrenmon-in]] * [[:en:Taira no Tokiko|Nii No Ama]] "Suiten" is the [[:en:Japan|Japan]]ese name of the deity of [[:en:Hindus|Hindus]] origins [[:en:Varuna|Varuna]], one of a series of Hindu deities whose worship entered Japan together with [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]].{{refn|group=note|Varuna is one among a number of Hindu deities incorporated into [[Shinto]], introduced into Japan together with Buddhism: Indra (Jap. ''Taishakuten''), Agni (''Katen''), Yama (''Emmaten''), Nirrti (''Rasetsuten''), Vayu (''Futen''), Ishana (''Ishanaten''), Kubera (''Tamonten''), Brahma (''Bonten''), Prithvi (''Chiten''), Surya (''Nitten''), Chandra (''Gatten'').Willem Frederik Stutterheim et al (1995), Rāma-legends and Rāma-reliefs in Indonesia, {{ISBN|978-8170172512}}, pages xiv–xviS Biswas (2000), Art of Japan, Northern, {{ISBN|978-8172112691}}, page 184[[:en:Adrian Snodgrass|Adrian Snodgrass]] (2007), The Symbolism of the Stupa, Motilal Banarsidass, {{ISBN|978-8120807815}}, pages 120-124, 298-300}}. When the Japanese Empire enforced the ''[[Shinbutsu bunri]]'', the official separation of Shinto shrines and Buddhist temples, shrines celebrating Suiten identified their dedication to [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]]."Tokyo Suitengu monogatari" 1985 Kodansha, {{ISBN|406202117X}} Suitengu is located in [[:en:Chūō, Tokyo|Chūō, Tokyo]]. It is devoted to conception and safe childbirth. In 1818 the ninth [[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]] of the [[:en:Kurume Domain|Kurume Domain]] established the Suitengu in [[:en:Edo|Edo]] as a branch of a shrine of the same name in [[:en:Kurume|Kurume]]. It was inside the grounds of the domain's mansion in the Mita district of what is now [[:en:Minato, Tokyo|Minato, Tokyo]], and the domain opened it to the public on the fifth day of every month. In 1871, the [[:en:Arima clan|Arima family]] moved from Mita to [[:en:Akasaka, Tokyo|Akasaka]], taking the shrine with them, and in the following year they moved the shrine to its present location, on a site that had been occupied by one of the family's mansions. [[:en:Suitengūmae Station|Suitengūmae Station]] is close to this shrine and takes its name from it. There are about twenty-five other shrines of the same name in Japan. ==See also== *[[Kurume Suitengū]] *[[Suijin]] ==Notes== {{reflist|group=note}} ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== *[http://www.suitengu.or.jp/ Official site] (in Japanese) {{Chuo, Tokyo}} ]] {{Shinto-stub}} {{draft categories| [[Category:1818 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Chūō, Tokyo]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Unranked shrines]] [[Category:Suiten-gū Shrines]] }} [[en:Suitengū (Tokyo)]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Suiten-gū (Chuo, Tokyo)}} [[de:Suiten-gū (Tokio)]] [[fr:Suiten-gū (Tokyo)]] [[ja:水天宮 (東京都中央区)]] [[th:ศาลเจ้าซุยเต็น (โตเกียว)]] [[zh:水天宮 (東京都中央區)]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]] {{wikidata link|Q3503320}}

[[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]] 103-0014 | website = {{URL|http://www.suitengu.or.jp/}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Suiten-gū'''|水天宮}}, literally "Palace of the Water [[:en:Deva (Buddhism)|Deva]]", or "Palace of Suiten", is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] dedicated to four deities: * [[Ame-no-Minakanushi|Amenominakanushi]] * [[:en:Emperor Antoku|Antoku]] * [[Taira no Tokuko|Kenrenmon-in]] * [[:en:Taira no Tokiko|Nii No Ama]] "Suiten" is the [[:en:Japan|Japan]]ese name of the deity of [[:en:Hindus|Hindus]] origins [[:en:Varuna|Varuna]], one of a series of Hindu deities whose worship entered Japan together with [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]].{{refn|group=note|Varuna is one among a number of Hindu deities incorporated into [[Shinto]], introduced into Japan together with Buddhism: Indra (Jap. ''Taishakuten''), Agni (''Katen''), Yama (''Emmaten''), Nirrti (''Rasetsuten''), Vayu (''Futen''), Ishana (''Ishanaten''), Kubera (''Tamonten''), Brahma (''Bonten''), Prithvi (''Chiten''), Surya (''Nitten''), Chandra (''Gatten'').Willem Frederik Stutterheim et al (1995), Rāma-legends and Rāma-reliefs in Indonesia, {{ISBN|978-8170172512}}, pages xiv–xviS Biswas (2000), Art of Japan, Northern, {{ISBN|978-8172112691}}, page 184[[:en:Adrian Snodgrass|Adrian Snodgrass]] (2007), The Symbolism of the Stupa, Motilal Banarsidass, {{ISBN|978-8120807815}}, pages 120-124, 298-300}}. When the Japanese Empire enforced the ''[[Shinbutsu bunri]]'', the official separation of Shinto shrines and Buddhist temples, shrines celebrating Suiten identified their dedication to [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]]."Tokyo Suitengu monogatari" 1985 Kodansha, {{ISBN|406202117X}} Suitengu is located in [[:en:Chūō, Tokyo|Chūō, Tokyo]]. It is devoted to conception and safe childbirth. In 1818 the ninth [[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]] of the [[:en:Kurume Domain|Kurume Domain]] established the Suitengu in [[:en:Edo|Edo]] as a branch of a shrine of the same name in [[:en:Kurume|Kurume]]. It was inside the grounds of the domain's mansion in the Mita district of what is now [[:en:Minato, Tokyo|Minato, Tokyo]], and the domain opened it to the public on the fifth day of every month. In 1871, the [[:en:Arima clan|Arima family]] moved from Mita to [[:en:Akasaka, Tokyo|Akasaka]], taking the shrine with them, and in the following year they moved the shrine to its present location, on a site that had been occupied by one of the family's mansions. [[:en:Suitengūmae Station|Suitengūmae Station]] is close to this shrine and takes its name from it. There are about twenty-five other shrines of the same name in Japan. ==See also== *[[Kurume Suitengū]] *[[Suijin]] ==Notes== {{reflist|group=note}} ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== *[http://www.suitengu.or.jp/ Official site] (in Japanese) {{Chuo, Tokyo}} ]] {{Shinto-stub}} {{draft categories| [[Category:1818 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Chūō, Tokyo]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Unranked shrines]] [[Category:Suiten-gū Shrines]] }} [[en:Suitengū (Tokyo)]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Suiten-gū (Chuo, Tokyo)}} [[de:Suiten-gū (Tokio)]] [[fr:Suiten-gū (Tokyo)]] [[ja:水天宮 (東京都中央区)]] [[th:ศาลเจ้าซุยเต็น (โตเกียว)]] [[zh:水天宮 (東京都中央區)]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] {{wikidata link|Q3503320}}

''Taishakuten''), Agni (''Katen''), Yama (''Emmaten''), Nirrti (''Rasetsuten''), Vayu (''Futen''), Ishana (''Ishanaten''), Kubera (''Tamonten''), Brahma (''Bonten''), Prithvi (''Chiten''), Surya (''Nitten''), Chandra (''Gatten'').Willem Frederik Stutterheim et al (1995), Rāma-legends and Rāma-reliefs in Indonesia, {{ISBN|978-8170172512}}, pages xiv–xviS Biswas (2000), Art of Japan, Northern, {{ISBN|978-8172112691}}, page 184[[:en:Adrian Snodgrass|Adrian Snodgrass]] (2007), The Symbolism of the Stupa, Motilal Banarsidass, {{ISBN|978-8120807815}}, pages 120-124, 298-300}}. When the Japanese Empire enforced the ''[[Shinbutsu bunri]]'', the official separation of Shinto shrines and Buddhist temples, shrines celebrating Suiten identified their dedication to [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]]."Tokyo Suitengu monogatari" 1985 Kodansha, {{ISBN|406202117X}} Suitengu is located in [[:en:Chūō, Tokyo|Chūō, Tokyo]]. It is devoted to conception and safe childbirth. In 1818 the ninth [[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]] of the [[:en:Kurume Domain|Kurume Domain]] established the Suitengu in [[:en:Edo|Edo]] as a branch of a shrine of the same name in [[:en:Kurume|Kurume]]. It was inside the grounds of the domain's mansion in the Mita district of what is now [[:en:Minato, Tokyo|Minato, Tokyo]], and the domain opened it to the public on the fifth day of every month. In 1871, the [[:en:Arima clan|Arima family]] moved from Mita to [[:en:Akasaka, Tokyo|Akasaka]], taking the shrine with them, and in the following year they moved the shrine to its present location, on a site that had been occupied by one of the family's mansions. [[:en:Suitengūmae Station|Suitengūmae Station]] is close to this shrine and takes its name from it. There are about twenty-five other shrines of the same name in Japan. ==See also== *[[Kurume Suitengū]] *[[Suijin]] ==Notes== {{reflist|group=note}} ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== *[http://www.suitengu.or.jp/ Official site] (in Japanese) {{Chuo, Tokyo}} ]] {{Shinto-stub}} {{draft categories| }} [[en:Suitengū (Tokyo)]] {{Commons category|Suiten-gū (Chuo, Tokyo)}} [[de:Suiten-gū (Tokio)]] [[fr:Suiten-gū (Tokyo)]] [[ja:水天宮 (東京都中央区)]] [[th:ศาลเจ้าซุยเต็น (โตเกียว)]] [[zh:水天宮 (東京都中央區)]] {{wikidata link|Q3503320}} [[Category:1818 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Chūō, Tokyo]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Suiten-gū Shrines]] [[Category:Unranked shrines]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:pages with conflicting qids]]

''Taishakuten''), Agni (''Katen''), Yama (''Emmaten''), Nirrti (''Rasetsuten''), Vayu (''Futen''), Ishana (''Ishanaten''), Kubera (''Tamonten''), Brahma (''Bonten''), Prithvi (''Chiten''), Surya (''Nitten''), Chandra (''Gatten'').Willem Frederik Stutterheim et al (1995), Rāma-legends and Rāma-reliefs in Indonesia, {{ISBN|978-8170172512}}, pages xiv–xviS Biswas (2000), Art of Japan, Northern, {{ISBN|978-8172112691}}, page 184[[:en:Adrian Snodgrass|Adrian Snodgrass]] (2007), The Symbolism of the Stupa, Motilal Banarsidass, {{ISBN|978-8120807815}}, pages 120-124, 298-300}}. When the Japanese Empire enforced the ''[[Shinbutsu bunri]]'', the official separation of Shinto shrines and Buddhist temples, shrines celebrating Suiten identified their dedication to [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]]."Tokyo Suitengu monogatari" 1985 Kodansha, {{ISBN|406202117X}} Suitengu is located in [[:en:Chūō, Tokyo|Chūō, Tokyo]]. It is devoted to conception and safe childbirth. In 1818 the ninth [[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]] of the [[:en:Kurume Domain|Kurume Domain]] established the Suitengu in [[:en:Edo|Edo]] as a branch of a shrine of the same name in [[:en:Kurume|Kurume]]. It was inside the grounds of the domain's mansion in the Mita district of what is now [[:en:Minato, Tokyo|Minato, Tokyo]], and the domain opened it to the public on the fifth day of every month. In 1871, the [[:en:Arima clan|Arima family]] moved from Mita to [[:en:Akasaka, Tokyo|Akasaka]], taking the shrine with them, and in the following year they moved the shrine to its present location, on a site that had been occupied by one of the family's mansions. [[:en:Suitengūmae Station|Suitengūmae Station]] is close to this shrine and takes its name from it. There are about twenty-five other shrines of the same name in Japan. ==See also== *[[Kurume Suitengū]] *[[Suijin]] ==Notes== {{reflist|group=note}} ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== *[http://www.suitengu.or.jp/ Official site] (in Japanese) {{Chuo, Tokyo}} ]] {{Shinto-stub}} [[en:Suitengū (Tokyo)]] {{Commons category|Suiten-gū (Chuo, Tokyo)}} [[de:Suiten-gū (Tokio)]] [[fr:Suiten-gū (Tokyo)]] [[ja:水天宮 (東京都中央区)]] [[th:ศาลเจ้าซุยเต็น (โตเกียว)]] [[zh:水天宮 (東京都中央區)]] {{wikidata link|Q3503320}} [[Category:1818 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Chūō, Tokyo]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Suiten-gū Shrines]] [[Category:Unranked shrines]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Qq]]

{{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|WD=Q1490|lt=Tokyo}} 103-0014 | website = {{URL|http://www.suitengu.or.jp/}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Suiten-gū'''|水天宮}}, literally "Palace of the Water {{ill|Deva (Buddhism)|en|Deva (Buddhism)|WD=Q4089509|lt=Deva}}", or "Palace of Suiten", is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} dedicated to four deities: * [[Ame-no-Minakanushi|Amenominakanushi]] * {{ill|Emperor Antoku|en|Emperor Antoku|WD=Q316651|lt=Antoku}} * [[Taira no Tokuko|Kenrenmon-in]] * {{ill|Taira no Tokiko|en|Taira no Tokiko|WD=Q3379244|lt=Nii No Ama}} "Suiten" is the {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}ese name of the deity of {{ill|Hindus|en|Hindus|WD=Q10090|lt=Hindus}} origins {{ill|Varuna|en|Varuna|WD=Q1001037|lt=Varuna}}, one of a series of Hindu deities whose worship entered Japan together with {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|WD=Q748|lt=Buddhism}}.{{refn|group=note|Varuna is one among a number of Hindu deities incorporated into [[Shinto]], introduced into Japan together with Buddhism: Indra (Jap. ''Taishakuten''), Agni (''Katen''), Yama (''Emmaten''), Nirrti (''Rasetsuten''), Vayu (''Futen''), Ishana (''Ishanaten''), Kubera (''Tamonten''), Brahma (''Bonten''), Prithvi (''Chiten''), Surya (''Nitten''), Chandra (''Gatten'').Willem Frederik Stutterheim et al (1995), Rāma-legends and Rāma-reliefs in Indonesia, {{ISBN|978-8170172512}}, pages xiv–xviS Biswas (2000), Art of Japan, Northern, {{ISBN|978-8172112691}}, page 184{{ill|Adrian Snodgrass|en|Adrian Snodgrass|WD=Q4685366|lt=Adrian Snodgrass}} (2007), The Symbolism of the Stupa, Motilal Banarsidass, {{ISBN|978-8120807815}}, pages 120-124, 298-300}}. When the Japanese Empire enforced the ''[[Shinbutsu bunri]]'', the official separation of Shinto shrines and Buddhist temples, shrines celebrating Suiten identified their dedication to [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]]."Tokyo Suitengu monogatari" 1985 Kodansha, {{ISBN|406202117X}} Suitengu is located in {{ill|Chūō, Tokyo|en|Chūō, Tokyo|WD=Q212704|lt=Chūō, Tokyo}}. It is devoted to conception and safe childbirth. In 1818 the ninth [[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]] of the {{ill|Kurume Domain|en|Kurume Domain|WD=Q426826|lt=Kurume Domain}} established the Suitengu in {{ill|Edo|en|Edo|WD=Q215646|lt=Edo}} as a branch of a shrine of the same name in {{ill|Kurume|en|Kurume|WD=Q320934|lt=Kurume}}. It was inside the grounds of the domain's mansion in the Mita district of what is now {{ill|Minato, Tokyo|en|Minato, Tokyo|WD=Q190088|lt=Minato, Tokyo}}, and the domain opened it to the public on the fifth day of every month. In 1871, the [[:en:Arima clan|Arima family]] moved from Mita to {{ill|Akasaka, Tokyo|en|Akasaka, Tokyo|WD=Q416131|lt=Akasaka}}, taking the shrine with them, and in the following year they moved the shrine to its present location, on a site that had been occupied by one of the family's mansions. {{ill|Suitengūmae Station|en|Suitengūmae Station|WD=Q80546|lt=Suitengūmae Station}} is close to this shrine and takes its name from it. There are about twenty-five other shrines of the same name in Japan. ==See also== *[[Kurume Suitengū]] *[[Suijin]] ==Notes== {{reflist|group=note}} ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== *[http://www.suitengu.or.jp/ Official site] (in Japanese) {{Chuo, Tokyo}} ]] {{Shinto-stub}} [[en:Suitengū (Tokyo)]] {{Commons category|Suiten-gū (Chuo, Tokyo)}} [[de:Suiten-gū (Tokio)]] [[fr:Suiten-gū (Tokyo)]] [[ja:水天宮 (東京都中央区)]] [[th:ศาลเจ้าซุยเต็น (โตเกียว)]] [[zh:水天宮 (東京都中央區)]] {{wikidata link|Q3503320}} [[Category:1818 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Chūō, Tokyo]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Suiten-gū Shrines]] [[Category:Unranked shrines]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages with unclosed square brackets]]

In 1871, the [[:en:Arima clan|Arima family]] moved from Mita to {{ill|Akasaka, Tokyo|en|Akasaka, Tokyo|WD=Q416131|lt=Akasaka}}, taking the shrine with them, and in the following year they moved the shrine to its present location, on a site that had been occupied by one of the family's mansions. {{ill|Suitengūmae Station|en|Suitengūmae Station|WD=Q80546|lt=Suitengūmae Station}} is close to this shrine and takes its name from it. [[Taira no Tokuko]]
{{ill|Taira no Tokiko|en|Taira no Tokiko|WD=Q3379244|lt=Taira no Tokiko}} | founded_by = | established = 1818 | date_destroyed = | location = 2-4-1 Nihonbashi-Kakigarachō, {{ill|Chūō, Tokyo|en|Chūō, Tokyo|WD=Q212704|lt=Chūō}}
{{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|WD=Q1490|lt=Tokyo}} 103-0014 | website = {{URL|http://www.suitengu.or.jp/}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Suiten-gū'''|水天宮}}, literally "Palace of the Water {{ill|Deva (Buddhism)|en|Deva (Buddhism)|WD=Q4089509|lt=Deva}}", or "Palace of Suiten", is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} dedicated to four deities: * [[Ame-no-Minakanushi|Amenominakanushi]] * {{ill|Emperor Antoku|en|Emperor Antoku|WD=Q316651|lt=Antoku}} * [[Taira no Tokuko|Kenrenmon-in]] * {{ill|Taira no Tokiko|en|Taira no Tokiko|WD=Q3379244|lt=Nii No Ama}} "Suiten" is the {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}ese name of the deity of {{ill|Hindus|en|Hindus|WD=Q10090|lt=Hindus}} origins {{ill|Varuna|en|Varuna|WD=Q1001037|lt=Varuna}}, one of a series of Hindu deities whose worship entered Japan together with {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|WD=Q748|lt=Buddhism}}.{{refn|group=note|Varuna is one among a number of Hindu deities incorporated into [[Shinto]], introduced into Japan together with Buddhism: Indra (Jap. ''Taishakuten''), Agni (''Katen''), Yama (''Emmaten''), Nirrti (''Rasetsuten''), Vayu (''Futen''), Ishana (''Ishanaten''), Kubera (''Tamonten''), Brahma (''Bonten''), Prithvi (''Chiten''), Surya (''Nitten''), Chandra (''Gatten'').Willem Frederik Stutterheim et al (1995), Rāma-legends and Rāma-reliefs in Indonesia, {{ISBN|978-8170172512}}, pages xiv–xviS Biswas (2000), Art of Japan, Northern, {{ISBN|978-8172112691}}, page 184{{ill|Adrian Snodgrass|en|Adrian Snodgrass|WD=Q4685366|lt=Adrian Snodgrass}} (2007), The Symbolism of the Stupa, Motilal Banarsidass, {{ISBN|978-8120807815}}, pages 120-124, 298-300}}. When the Japanese Empire enforced the ''[[Shinbutsu bunri]]'', the official separation of Shinto shrines and Buddhist temples, shrines celebrating Suiten identified their dedication to [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]]."Tokyo Suitengu monogatari" 1985 Kodansha, {{ISBN|406202117X}} Suitengu is located in {{ill|Chūō, Tokyo|en|Chūō, Tokyo|WD=Q212704|lt=Chūō, Tokyo}}. It is devoted to conception and safe childbirth. In 1818 the ninth [[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]] of the {{ill|Kurume Domain|en|Kurume Domain|WD=Q426826|lt=Kurume Domain}} established the Suitengu in {{ill|Edo|en|Edo|WD=Q215646|lt=Edo}} as a branch of a shrine of the same name in {{ill|Kurume|en|Kurume|WD=Q320934|lt=Kurume}}. It was inside the grounds of the domain's mansion in the Mita district of what is now {{ill|Minato, Tokyo|en|Minato, Tokyo|WD=Q190088|lt=Minato, Tokyo}}, and the domain opened it to the public on the fifth day of every month. In 1871, the [[:en:Arima clan|Arima family]] moved from Mita to {{ill|Akasaka, Tokyo|en|Akasaka, Tokyo|WD=Q416131|lt=Akasaka}}, taking the shrine with them, and in the following year they moved the shrine to its present location, on a site that had been occupied by one of the family's mansions. {{ill|Suitengūmae Station|en|Suitengūmae Station|WD=Q80546|lt=Suitengūmae Station}} is close to this shrine and takes its name from it. There are about twenty-five other shrines of the same name in Japan. ==See also== *[[Kurume Suitengū]] *[[Suijin]] ==Notes== {{reflist|group=note}} ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== *[http://www.suitengu.or.jp/ Official site] (in Japanese) {{Chuo, Tokyo}} ]] {{Shinto-stub}} [[en:Suitengū (Tokyo)]] {{Commons category|Suiten-gū (Chuo, Tokyo)}} [[de:Suiten-gū (Tokio)]] [[fr:Suiten-gū (Tokyo)]] [[ja:水天宮 (東京都中央区)]] [[th:ศาลเจ้าซุยเต็น (โตเกียว)]] [[zh:水天宮 (東京都中央區)]] {{wikidata link|Q3503320}} [[Category:1818 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Chūō, Tokyo]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Suiten-gū Shrines]] [[Category:Unranked shrines]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages with unclosed square brackets]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]]

{{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|WD=Q1490|lt=Tokyo|1=Tokyo}} 103-0014 | website = {{URL|http://www.suitengu.or.jp/}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Suiten-gū'''|水天宮}}, literally "Palace of the Water {{ill|Deva (Buddhism)|en|Deva (Buddhism)|WD=Q4089509|lt=Deva|1=Deva (Buddhism)}}", or "Palace of Suiten", is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} dedicated to four deities: * [[Ame-no-Minakanushi|Amenominakanushi]] * {{ill|Emperor Antoku|en|Emperor Antoku|WD=Q316651|lt=Antoku|1=Emperor Antoku}} * [[Taira no Tokuko|Kenrenmon-in]] * {{ill|Taira no Tokiko|en|Taira no Tokiko|WD=Q3379244|lt=Nii No Ama|1=Taira no Tokiko}} "Suiten" is the {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}ese name of the deity of {{ill|Hindus|en|Hindus|WD=Q10090|lt=Hindus|1=Hindus}} origins {{ill|Varuna|en|Varuna|WD=Q1001037|lt=Varuna|1=Varuna}}, one of a series of Hindu deities whose worship entered Japan together with {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|WD=Q748|lt=Buddhism|1=Buddhism}}.{{refn|group=note|Varuna is one among a number of Hindu deities incorporated into [[Shinto]], introduced into Japan together with Buddhism: Indra (Jap. ''Taishakuten''), Agni (''Katen''), Yama (''Emmaten''), Nirrti (''Rasetsuten''), Vayu (''Futen''), Ishana (''Ishanaten''), Kubera (''Tamonten''), Brahma (''Bonten''), Prithvi (''Chiten''), Surya (''Nitten''), Chandra (''Gatten'').Willem Frederik Stutterheim et al (1995), Rāma-legends and Rāma-reliefs in Indonesia, {{ISBN|978-8170172512}}, pages xiv–xviS Biswas (2000), Art of Japan, Northern, {{ISBN|978-8172112691}}, page 184{{ill|Adrian Snodgrass|en|Adrian Snodgrass|WD=Q4685366|lt=Adrian Snodgrass|1=Adrian Snodgrass}} (2007), The Symbolism of the Stupa, Motilal Banarsidass, {{ISBN|978-8120807815}}, pages 120-124, 298-300}}. When the Japanese Empire enforced the ''[[Shinbutsu bunri]]'', the official separation of Shinto shrines and Buddhist temples, shrines celebrating Suiten identified their dedication to [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]]."Tokyo Suitengu monogatari" 1985 Kodansha, {{ISBN|406202117X}} Suitengu is located in {{ill|Chūō, Tokyo|en|Chūō, Tokyo|WD=Q212704|lt=Chūō, Tokyo|1=Chūō, Tokyo}}. It is devoted to conception and safe childbirth. In 1818 the ninth [[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]] of the {{ill|Kurume Domain|en|Kurume Domain|WD=Q426826|lt=Kurume Domain|1=Kurume Domain}} established the Suitengu in {{ill|Edo|en|Edo|WD=Q215646|lt=Edo|1=Edo}} as a branch of a shrine of the same name in {{ill|Kurume|en|Kurume|WD=Q320934|lt=Kurume|1=Kurume}}. It was inside the grounds of the domain's mansion in the Mita district of what is now {{ill|Minato, Tokyo|en|Minato, Tokyo|WD=Q190088|lt=Minato, Tokyo|1=Minato, Tokyo}}, and the domain opened it to the public on the fifth day of every month. In 1871, the [[:en:Arima clan|Arima family]] moved from Mita to {{ill|Akasaka, Tokyo|en|Akasaka, Tokyo|WD=Q416131|lt=Akasaka|1=Akasaka, Tokyo}}, taking the shrine with them, and in the following year they moved the shrine to its present location, on a site that had been occupied by one of the family's mansions. {{ill|Suitengūmae Station|en|Suitengūmae Station|WD=Q80546|lt=Suitengūmae Station|1=Suitengūmae Station}} is close to this shrine and takes its name from it. There are about twenty-five other shrines of the same name in Japan. ==See also== *[[Kurume Suitengū]] *[[Suijin]] ==Notes== {{reflist|group=note}} ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== *[http://www.suitengu.or.jp/ Official site] (in Japanese) {{Chuo, Tokyo}} ]] {{Shinto-stub}} [[en:Suitengū (Tokyo)]] {{Commons category|Suiten-gū (Chuo, Tokyo)}} [[de:Suiten-gū (Tokio)]] [[fr:Suiten-gū (Tokyo)]] [[ja:水天宮 (東京都中央区)]] [[th:ศาลเจ้าซุยเต็น (โตเกียว)]] [[zh:水天宮 (東京都中央區)]] {{wikidata link|Q3503320}} [[Category:1818 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Chūō, Tokyo]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Suiten-gū Shrines]] [[Category:Unranked shrines]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages with unclosed square brackets]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:pages]]

In 1818 the ninth [[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]] of the {{ill|Kurume Domain|en|Kurume Domain|WD=Q426826|lt=Kurume Domain|1=Kurume Domain}} established the Suitengu in {{ill|Edo|en|Edo|WD=Q215646|lt=Edo|1=Edo}} as a branch of a shrine of the same name in {{ill|Kurume|en|Kurume|WD=Q320934|lt=Kurume|1=Kurume}}. It was inside the grounds of the domain's mansion in the Mita district of what is now {{ill|Minato, Tokyo|en|Minato, Tokyo|WD=Q190088|lt=Minato, Tokyo|1=Minato, Tokyo}}, and the domain opened it to the public on the fifth day of every month. In 1871, the [[:en:Arima clan|Arima family]] moved from Mita to {{ill|Akasaka, Tokyo|en|Akasaka, Tokyo|WD=Q416131|lt=Akasaka|1=Akasaka, Tokyo}}, taking the shrine with them, and in the following year they moved the shrine to its present location, on a site that had been occupied by one of the family's mansions. {{ill|Suitengūmae Station|en|Suitengūmae Station|WD=Q80546|lt=Suitengūmae Station|1=Suitengūmae Station}} is close to this shrine and takes its name from it. There are about twenty-five other shrines of the same name in Japan. ==See also== *[[Kurume Suitengū]] *[[Suijin]] ==Notes== {{reflist|group=note}} ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== *[http://www.suitengu.or.jp/ Official site] (in Japanese) {{Chuo, Tokyo}} ]] {{Shinto-stub}} [[en:Suitengū (Tokyo)]] {{Commons category|Suiten-gū (Chuo, Tokyo)}} [[de:Suiten-gū (Tokio)]] [[fr:Suiten-gū (Tokyo)]] [[ja:水天宮 (東京都中央区)]] [[th:ศาลเจ้าซุยเต็น (โตเกียว)]] [[zh:水天宮 (東京都中央區)]] {{wikidata link|Q3503320}} [[Category:1818 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Chūō, Tokyo]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Suiten-gū Shrines]] [[Category:Unranked shrines]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

水天宮 | image = Tokyo Suitengu 201604a.jpg | caption = The current shrine (Rebuilt in 2016) | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|41|01|N|139|47|06|E|region:JP_scale:30000_source:jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]]（as {{ill|Varuna|en|Varuna|WD=Q1001037|lt=Varuna|1=Varuna}}）
{{ill|Emperor Antoku|en|Emperor Antoku|WD=Q316651|lt=Emperor Antoku|1=Emperor Antoku}}
[[Taira no Tokuko]]
{{ill|Taira no Tokiko|en|Taira no Tokiko|WD=Q3379244|lt=Taira no Tokiko|1=Taira no Tokiko}} | founded_by = | established = 1818 | date_destroyed = | location = 2-4-1 Nihonbashi-Kakigarachō, {{ill|Chūō, Tokyo|en|Chūō, Tokyo|WD=Q212704|lt=Chūō|1=Chūō, Tokyo}}
{{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|WD=Q1490|lt=Tokyo|1=Tokyo}} 103-0014 | website = {{URL|http://www.suitengu.or.jp/}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Suiten-gū'''|水天宮}}, literally "Palace of the Water {{ill|Deva (Buddhism)|en|Deva (Buddhism)|WD=Q4089509|lt=Deva|1=Deva (Buddhism)}}", or "Palace of Suiten", is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} dedicated to four deities: * [[Ame-no-Minakanushi|Amenominakanushi]] * {{ill|Emperor Antoku|en|Emperor Antoku|WD=Q316651|lt=Antoku|1=Emperor Antoku}} * [[Taira no Tokuko|Kenrenmon-in]] * {{ill|Taira no Tokiko|en|Taira no Tokiko|WD=Q3379244|lt=Nii No Ama|1=Taira no Tokiko}} "Suiten" is the {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}ese name of the deity of {{ill|Hindus|en|Hindus|WD=Q10090|lt=Hindus|1=Hindus}} origins {{ill|Varuna|en|Varuna|WD=Q1001037|lt=Varuna|1=Varuna}}, one of a series of Hindu deities whose worship entered Japan together with {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|WD=Q748|lt=Buddhism|1=Buddhism}}.{{refn|group=note|Varuna is one among a number of Hindu deities incorporated into [[Shinto]], introduced into Japan together with Buddhism: Indra (Jap. ''Taishakuten''), Agni (''Katen''), Yama (''Emmaten''), Nirrti (''Rasetsuten''), Vayu (''Futen''), Ishana (''Ishanaten''), Kubera (''Tamonten''), Brahma (''Bonten''), Prithvi (''Chiten''), Surya (''Nitten''), Chandra (''Gatten'').Willem Frederik Stutterheim et al (1995), Rāma-legends and Rāma-reliefs in Indonesia, {{ISBN|978-8170172512}}, pages xiv–xviS Biswas (2000), Art of Japan, Northern, {{ISBN|978-8172112691}}, page 184{{ill|Adrian Snodgrass|en|Adrian Snodgrass|WD=Q4685366|lt=Adrian Snodgrass|1=Adrian Snodgrass}} (2007), The Symbolism of the Stupa, Motilal Banarsidass, {{ISBN|978-8120807815}}, pages 120-124, 298-300}}. When the Japanese Empire enforced the ''[[Shinbutsu bunri]]'', the official separation of Shinto shrines and Buddhist temples, shrines celebrating Suiten identified their dedication to [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]]."Tokyo Suitengu monogatari" 1985 Kodansha, {{ISBN|406202117X}} Suitengu is located in {{ill|Chūō, Tokyo|en|Chūō, Tokyo|WD=Q212704|lt=Chūō, Tokyo|1=Chūō, Tokyo}}. It is devoted to conception and safe childbirth. In 1818 the ninth [[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]] of the {{ill|Kurume Domain|en|Kurume Domain|WD=Q426826|lt=Kurume Domain|1=Kurume Domain}} established the Suitengu in {{ill|Edo|en|Edo|WD=Q215646|lt=Edo|1=Edo}} as a branch of a shrine of the same name in {{ill|Kurume|en|Kurume|WD=Q320934|lt=Kurume|1=Kurume}}. It was inside the grounds of the domain's mansion in the Mita district of what is now {{ill|Minato, Tokyo|en|Minato, Tokyo|WD=Q190088|lt=Minato, Tokyo|1=Minato, Tokyo}}, and the domain opened it to the public on the fifth day of every month. In 1871, the [[:en:Arima clan|Arima family]] moved from Mita to {{ill|Akasaka, Tokyo|en|Akasaka, Tokyo|WD=Q416131|lt=Akasaka|1=Akasaka, Tokyo}}, taking the shrine with them, and in the following year they moved the shrine to its present location, on a site that had been occupied by one of the family's mansions. {{ill|Suitengūmae Station|en|Suitengūmae Station|WD=Q80546|lt=Suitengūmae Station|1=Suitengūmae Station}} is close to this shrine and takes its name from it. There are about twenty-five other shrines of the same name in Japan. ==See also== *[[Kurume Suitengū]] *[[Suijin]] ==Notes== {{reflist|group=note}} ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== *[http://www.suitengu.or.jp/ Official site] (in Japanese) {{Chuo, Tokyo}} ]] {{Shinto-stub}} [[en:Suitengū (Tokyo)]] {{Commons category|Suiten-gū (Chuo, Tokyo)}} [[de:Suiten-gū (Tokio)]] [[fr:Suiten-gū (Tokyo)]] [[ja:水天宮 (東京都中央区)]] [[th:ศาลเจ้าซุยเต็น (โตเกียว)]] [[zh:水天宮 (東京都中央區)]] {{wikidata link|Q3503320}} [[Category:1818 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Chūō, Tokyo]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Suiten-gū Shrines]] [[Category:Unranked shrines]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]
